generatedFrom: standalone Notes Studio (Grade 7: Integrated Science + Social Studies)

blocks.text: Good revision is not just reading — it is **doing**. These tips will help you remember more and score better in Grade 7 Integrated Science (ECZ).
Before you start

items: Make a simple timetable and study a little every day, not everything the night before.
Find a quiet, well-lit place and keep your phone away.
Gather what you need: your notes, a pen, and blank paper for practice.

blocks.text: While studying a topic

items: Read a section, then **cover it and say or write** the main points from memory.
Turn headings into questions and try to answer them.
Draw and label the diagrams yourself — flowers, the digestive system, circuits, eclipses.
Make your own short lists and tables; the act of writing them helps you remember.

blocks.text: Memory tricks

items: Use a made-up sentence (mnemonic) to remember an order, e.g. the planets or the parts of the gut.
Group facts: viral vs bacterial diseases; metals vs non-metals; series vs parallel.
Always learn one clear **example** for each idea.

blocks.text: Practising questions

items: Do the quiz for each subtopic, then check your answers and re-read anything you got wrong.
In multiple-choice, read all four options before choosing.
Cross out the answers you know are wrong first, then choose from what is left.
Watch for the words NOT, EXCEPT and TRUE/FALSE — they change the meaning.

blocks.text: In the exam

items: Read each question slowly; underline the key word.
Answer the easy questions first, then come back to the hard ones.
Do not leave blanks — make your best choice.
Check your work if there is time.

lines: Health reminder: sleep well and eat before an exam. A rested brain remembers far more than a tired one.

items: Study a little each day and test yourself from memory.
Draw the diagrams and make your own tables.
Practise past questions and learn from your mistakes.
In the exam, read carefully, do the easy ones first, and never leave blanks.
Describe digestion.
Identify the organs of the digestive system.
Identify where digested food is absorbed.
Explain what happens to undigested food.

lines: What happens to the food you eat after you swallow it?
Why can't the body use food without changing it first?

blocks.text: We eat food so that our bodies can stay healthy and strong, but food cannot help the body directly — it first has to be broken down. **Digestion** is the chemical breaking down of food into simple substances the body can absorb. It takes place in the **digestive system** (also called the alimentary canal or gut), a long tube from the mouth to the anus.

rows.def: The chemical break-down of food into simple substances the body can absorb.
Taking food into the mouth.
Passing of digested food into the bloodstream.
Removing undigested food as faeces through the anus.
The long tube from the mouth to the anus where digestion happens.

blocks.text: Organs of the alimentary canal and what they do

cells: Teeth mash food; the tongue rolls it for swallowing. Starch begins to be broken into glucose.
Carries swallowed food to the stomach. No digestion here.
Gastric juices begin to break down proteins; hydrochloric acid kills germs.
Made of the duodenum and ileum. Digestion is completed and food is absorbed into the blood.
Made of the colon and rectum. Water and mineral salts are absorbed; the rectum stores faeces.
Undigested food (faeces) leaves the body here — egestion.

blocks.text: Helper organs (make juices but food does not pass through them)

items: **Liver** — produces bile, which breaks down fats and oils. Bile is stored in the gall bladder.
**Pancreas** — produces pancreatic juice that further breaks down proteins and carbohydrates.

blocks.text: Diagram of the digestive system

lines: Path of food: Mouth → Oesophagus → Stomach → Small intestine → Large intestine → Anus.
Undigested food (mostly fibre) is stored in the rectum, then removed through the anus as faeces.
Remember the order of the gut with a sentence you invent, e.g. 'My Old Stomach Slowly Lets Air' = Mouth, Oesophagus, Stomach, Small intestine, Large intestine, Anus.
Be ready to point to the stomach, small intestine and liver on a diagram.

blocks.q: Where does the digestion of starch begin?
blocks.a: In the mouth.

blocks.q: In which organ is digestion completed and food absorbed?
blocks.a: The small intestine.

blocks.wrong: Food is absorbed in the stomach.
blocks.correct: Most digested food is absorbed in the small intestine.

blocks.q: State where the digestion of proteins begins.
blocks.a: Proteins are first digested in the stomach, where gastric juices act on them.

items: Digestion breaks food into substances the body can absorb.
Starch digestion begins in the mouth; protein digestion begins in the stomach.
Food is absorbed in the small intestine; water in the large intestine.
The liver makes bile (stored in the gall bladder); the pancreas makes pancreatic juice.
Distinguish between a virus and a bacterium.
Explain how viruses and bacteria affect health.
Identify common diseases of the skin.
Explain how health services affect how common diseases are.

lines: Have you or someone you know ever had cholera or measles?
Do you think all tiny germs are harmful?

rows.def: A tiny, non-living germ that causes disease; most have no cure.
Tiny living germs; some cause disease, many can be treated with antibiotics.
Giving a vaccine to protect against a disease.
Medicines that treat diseases caused by bacteria.

blocks.text: We stay healthy through good hygiene, a balanced diet and clean surroundings. Many diseases are caused by tiny **micro-organisms**. Two important ones are **viruses** and **bacteria**. Both can only be seen with a powerful microscope and both multiply very fast. (Remember: bacterium = one; bacteria = many.)
Differences between a virus and a bacterium

cells: Can live and survive on their own.
Most viral diseases have no cure.
Not all bacteria cause disease — some are useful.
Many controlled by immunisation and antibiotics.

blocks.text: How germs get into the body

items: Bites from insects (mostly bacteria).
Through the nose, by breathing contaminated air.
Through the mouth, by eating or drinking contaminated food or water.
Through the skin, or by body-to-body contact with an infected person.

blocks.text: Common diseases

cells: Waterborne. Vomiting, diarrhoea, dehydration; can kill.
Antibiotics; oral rehydration; clean water, hygiene.
Dirty food and water. Fever, stomach pains, red spots.
Airborne. Attacks lungs: cough with sputum, weight loss.
Unprotected sex, infected blood, needles, mother to child.
No cure/vaccine. ARVs control it; safe behaviour.
Airborne. Fever, cough, red eyes, rash behind ears.
No cure; vaccinate at about 9 months.
Airborne. Fever, sore muscles, paralysis of legs.
No cure; oral polio vaccine; good sanitation.
Very infectious, children. Itchy rash, fever.

blocks.text: Common skin diseases

items: **Ringworm** — caused by a fungus; inflamed patch, spread by contact.
**Scabies** — contagious rash caused by mites that burrow into the skin.
**Scurvy** — deficiency disease from lack of Vitamin C; gums swell and bleed.
**Chicken pox & warts** — caused by viruses.
**Skin cancer** — from too much UV radiation from the sun; prevent with sunscreen.

lines: Most viral diseases cannot be cured but can be prevented by immunisation. Most bacterial diseases can be treated with antibiotics. AIDS = Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome, caused by HIV.
First decide if a disease is viral (usually no cure, prevented by vaccine) or bacterial (often treated with antibiotics) — it answers many questions.
Learn which diseases are waterborne (cholera, typhoid) and which are airborne (TB, measles, flu, polio).

blocks.q: Name the virus that causes AIDS.
blocks.a: HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus).

blocks.q: Is cholera caused by a virus or by bacteria?
blocks.a: Bacteria.

blocks.wrong: Antibiotics can cure viral diseases like measles.
blocks.correct: Antibiotics treat bacterial diseases; most viral diseases are prevented by immunisation, not cured by antibiotics.

blocks.q: State two ways HIV can be transmitted.
blocks.a: Through unprotected sex with an infected person and through infected blood transfusion. (Also sharing needles, or mother to child.)

items: Viruses are non-living and mostly have no cure; bacteria are living and many are treatable.
Cholera and typhoid are waterborne; TB, measles, flu and polio are airborne.
Skin diseases: ringworm (fungus), scabies (mites), scurvy (lack of Vitamin C).
More health facilities reduce the spread of disease.
Identify fruits and seeds used as food.
State the importance of fruits in improving health.

lines: Which fruits grow naturally near your home?
Why do you feel healthier when you eat fruit?

rows.def: A fruit that grows naturally in the wild.
A fruit brought from another country but grown locally.
A crop with seeds in pods, rich in protein (e.g. beans).
A grain crop rich in carbohydrates (e.g. maize).

blocks.text: After a flower's ovary is fertilised it becomes a **fruit**; the ovules inside become seeds. Fruits are an important part of our diet because they contain many useful nutrients. Most are eaten raw, so we get all the minerals.
Fruits used as food

items: **Indigenous fruits** grow naturally in the wild — e.g. masuku, murula, mawi (monkey orange), manego (snot apple), mubuyu (baobab).
**Exotic fruits** come from other countries but are grown locally — e.g. mangoes, avocados, oranges, pawpaws, bananas, pineapples.

blocks.text: Seeds used as food

items: **Legumes** bear seeds in pods and have root nodules that fix nitrogen into the soil. Examples: beans, cowpeas, groundnuts, soya beans. Rich in **proteins**.
**Cereals (grain crops)** are grown for grains rich in **carbohydrates**. Examples: maize, sorghum, millet, rice, wheat.

blocks.text: Why fruits keep us healthy

items: Carbohydrates (fruit sugars) give energy.
Vitamins and minerals protect the body from disease.
Proteins build and repair cells.
Fibre helps digestion and prevents constipation.
Citrus fruits (oranges) are rich in Vitamin C, which prevents scurvy.
They are low in fat and contain a lot of water.

lines: Sick people, especially those who are HIV positive, should eat plenty of fruit — it tastes nice and is easy to eat even with a poor appetite.
Learn one clear example for each group: an indigenous fruit, an exotic fruit, a legume and a cereal — questions often ask you to pick the odd one out.

blocks.q: Which vitamin in oranges prevents scurvy?
blocks.a: Vitamin C.

blocks.q: Name the two main groups of seeds eaten as food.
blocks.a: Legumes and cereals.

blocks.wrong: An orange is a cereal.
blocks.correct: An orange is a fruit; cereals are grain crops like maize and millet.

blocks.q: State the main nutrient found in legumes.
blocks.a: Legumes are a good source of proteins, which build and repair the body.

items: A fruit develops from the ovary; seeds develop from ovules.
Indigenous fruits grow in the wild; exotic fruits come from other countries.
Legumes give mainly proteins; cereals give mainly carbohydrates.
Fruits supply energy, vitamins, minerals, proteins, fibre and water.
Separate an insoluble solid from water.
Separate a soluble substance from water.
Separate magnetic from non-magnetic materials.

lines: If salt and sand were mixed in water, how would you separate them?
Why does sand sink but salt seems to disappear?

blocks.text: When a **soluble** solid is put in a liquid it dissolves until it cannot be seen. Some solids do not dissolve; they are **insoluble**. The method we use to separate a mixture depends on the properties of the substances in it.

rows.def: The solid that dissolves in a liquid.
The liquid in which a solid dissolves.
The mixture formed when a solute dissolves in a solvent.
A solid that dissolves in a liquid (e.g. salt).
A solid that does not dissolve (e.g. sand).

blocks.text: Methods of separation

cells: Let the solid settle, then pour off the clear liquid.
Pour through a filter; solid (residue) stays, water passes through.
Heat until all water evaporates, leaving the solid behind.
A magnet attracts the iron filings, leaving the sand.

blocks.caption: Separating sand from water by decantation
lines: Sand and water are stirred in a cup, then left to stand.
The heavier sand settles at the bottom, leaving clear water on top.
The cup is tilted slowly and the clear water is poured off, leaving the sand behind.

blocks.text: Magnetic materials (attracted by a magnet) include iron nails, office pins and coins. Most materials are non-magnetic, e.g. rubber, pencils, plastic and clothes.

lines: Match the method to the clue word: dissolves → evaporation; does not dissolve → decantation or filtration; magnetic → magnet.

blocks.q: How do you recover salt from salty water?
blocks.a: By evaporation.

blocks.q: Which tool separates iron filings from sand?
blocks.a: A magnet.

blocks.wrong: Salt can be removed from water by filtration.
blocks.correct: Salt is dissolved (soluble), so it passes through a filter; use evaporation.

blocks.q: Name the method used to separate an insoluble solid like sand from water.
blocks.a: Decantation (or filtration).

items: Soluble solids dissolve to form a solution; insoluble solids do not.
Insoluble solids (sand) are separated by decantation or filtration.
Soluble solids (salt) are separated by evaporation.
A magnet separates magnetic materials (iron filings) from non-magnetic ones (sand).
Identify sources of water in villages and towns.
Identify types of water treatment.
State the importance of water treatment.
Describe ways of conserving water.

lines: Where does the water in your home come from?
Why must water be treated before we drink it?

rows.def: Making water safe to drink.
Adding chlorine to water to kill germs.
Boiling water and cooling the vapour to get pure water.
Preventing water from being lost, wasted or polluted.

blocks.text: Water makes up streams, rivers, lakes and oceans, and falls as rain. Most of the Earth's water is salty; only a small part is fresh, and most of that is locked up as ice and underground water.
Sources of water

items: **In villages:** wells, boreholes, springs; upland lakes and dams; rivers and streams; rain water.
**In towns:** the same sources, but water is transported (road or pipes), stored in tanks, and treated before use.

blocks.text: Methods of water treatment

cells: Kills germs, but does not remove chemicals.
Removes solid particles; germs and chemicals remain.
Lets mud settle; germs and chemicals remain.
Remove germs, colour and smell; chemicals remain.
Boil then cool the vapour. Removes chemicals, germs and minerals.
Add chlorine products (e.g. waterguard) to kill germs.
Clear bottles in the sun; the sun's rays kill most germs.

blocks.text: Urban water treatment — stages in order

items: Pumping from the source.
Storage in large tanks.
Screening to remove debris.
Pre-chlorination to kill germs.
Coagulation and flocculation (tiny particles clump).
Sedimentation and decantation.
Filtration of remaining particles.
Neutralising (adjust acidity; add fluoride for teeth).
Removal of excess mineral salts.
Disinfection (final chlorination).
Distribution to consumers.

blocks.text: Ways of conserving water

items: **Recycling** — treat used water and use it again.
**Reusing** — use water again for another job.
**Using sparingly** — bath from a basin, drip irrigation, turn off taps, fix leaks.
**Mulching** — cover soil with dry grass to reduce evaporation.
**Harvesting rain water** — collect rain from roof gutters.
**Afforestation** — plant trees to protect catchment areas.

lines: To get truly clean water you usually need two or more methods. Store treated water in clean, closed containers.
Learn the treatment stages as a flow chart and practise drawing it from memory — the order is a common question.

blocks.q: Which treatment method kills germs but not chemicals?
blocks.a: Boiling.

blocks.q: What is added to treated water to protect teeth?
blocks.a: Fluoride.

blocks.wrong: Boiling water removes poisonous chemicals.
blocks.correct: Boiling kills germs but does not remove dissolved chemicals.

blocks.q: State two ways of conserving water at home.
blocks.a: Reusing water (e.g. washing water to flush the toilet) and fixing leaking taps. (Also using a basin, harvesting rain.)

items: Town water must be treated to be safe.
Boiling kills germs; distillation removes chemicals, germs and minerals.
Water treatment protects us from disease.
Conserve water by recycling, reusing, using sparingly, mulching, harvesting rain and planting trees.
Identify the parts of a flower.
Explain the functions of the parts of a flower.

lines: Have you ever looked closely inside a flower?
Which parts do you think help the plant make seeds?

blocks.text: A flower is the **reproductive organ** of a plant — through it the plant produces seeds that grow into new plants. A flower has male parts, female parts, and other parts that are neither.
Diagram of a flower
Male, female and other parts

rows.def: Produce pollen grains (the male reproductive cells).
Receives pollen grains during pollination.
Tube joining the stigma to the ovary; the pollen tube grows down it.
Produces and protects the ovules; becomes a fruit after fertilisation.
The female reproductive cells; become seeds after fertilisation.
Often brightly coloured to attract insects.
Protect the bud and make food for the young flower.

lines: In a maize plant the male parts (tassels) are separate from the female parts. In a pawpaw plant the male and female flowers are on different plants.
Draw and label a flower from memory. Remember: stamen = male, pistil = female. The ovary becomes the fruit.

blocks.q: Which part receives pollen during pollination?
blocks.a: The stigma.

blocks.q: What does the ovary become after fertilisation?
blocks.a: A fruit.

blocks.wrong: The stamen is the female part of a flower.
blocks.correct: The stamen is the male part; the pistil is the female part.

blocks.q: Name the male parts of a flower.
blocks.a: The anthers, filaments and pollen grains, together called the stamen.

items: A flower is the reproductive organ of a plant.
Male parts = stamen; female parts = pistil.
The stigma receives pollen; the ovary becomes a fruit and ovules become seeds.
Petals attract insects; sepals protect the bud.
Describe pollination and identify its agents.
Describe fertilisation in flowering plants.

lines: How does pollen get from one flower to another?
What do you think bees do for flowers?

rows.def: The transfer of pollen from the anther to the stigma.
Pollen carried to a different plant of the same kind.
The joining of a pollen grain and an ovule.
The tube that carries the male cells to the ovary.

blocks.text: **Pollination** is the transfer of pollen grains from the anthers to the stigma of a flower.

items: **Self-pollination** — pollen moves to the stigma of the same flower (or same plant).
**Cross-pollination** — pollen moves to the stigma of a different plant of the same kind.

blocks.text: Agents of pollination

Agents carry pollen from the anthers to the stigma: **wind, water,** and **animals** such as insects and birds. Animals are attracted by bright petals and scent, and visit for nectar; pollen sticks to them and is carried to other flowers.
Fertilisation

**Fertilisation** is the joining (fusion) of a pollen grain and an ovule to form a **zygote**, which grows into a seed. It happens in the ovary. After pollen lands on the stigma, a pollen tube grows down through the style to the ovary.

blocks.caption: Pollen tube growing to the ovary
lines: A pollen grain rests on the stigma at the top.
A pollen tube grows down through the style towards the ovary.
Inside the ovary are the ovules, which the pollen grains reach and fertilise.

blocks.text: After fertilisation: the ovule becomes a seed and the ovary becomes a fruit. The petals, style, filament, stigma and anthers drop off; the sepals dry up later.

lines: Do not confuse the two: pollination (pollen to stigma) happens FIRST; fertilisation (pollen + ovule) happens after, inside the ovary.

blocks.q: What is pollination?
blocks.a: The transfer of pollen grains from the anther to the stigma.

blocks.q: Where does fertilisation take place in a plant?
blocks.a: In the ovary.

blocks.wrong: Pollination and fertilisation are the same thing.
blocks.correct: Pollination (pollen to stigma) happens first; fertilisation (pollen joins the ovule) happens after.

blocks.q: Name three agents of pollination.
blocks.a: Wind, water and animals (such as insects and birds).

items: Pollination = transfer of pollen from anther to stigma (self or cross).
Agents of pollination: wind, water, animals.
Fertilisation = fusion of a pollen grain and an ovule to form a zygote, in the ovary.
After fertilisation: ovule → seed, ovary → fruit.
Explain how fruits and seeds are produced.
Explain the importance of improving seed varieties.

lines: Why does a mango have one seed but a pawpaw has many?
What is the difference between a fruit and a seed?

rows.def: The part that develops from the ovary after fertilisation.
The part that develops from a fertilised ovule.
The outer covering of a fruit.
The seed coat (outer covering of a seed).

blocks.text: After fertilisation the ovary becomes a **fruit** and the ovules become **seeds**. Plants make many seeds so that those landing in good conditions can grow; this stops a species from becoming extinct. Seeds are also food for people and animals.
Differences between a fruit and a seed

cells: Outer covering is the testa (seed coat).

blocks.text: Importance of improving seed varieties

items: Improve yields (more profitable to farmers).
Withstand harsh conditions such as drought.
Produce crops of higher nutritional value.
Resist crop diseases.
Mature earlier than the parent seeds.

lines: Remember the scar trick: a fruit has two scars; a seed has one. Fruit from the ovary, seed from the ovule.

blocks.q: How many scars does a seed have?
blocks.a: One scar.

blocks.q: A seed develops from which part of the flower?
blocks.a: A fertilised ovule.

blocks.wrong: A fruit and a seed are the same thing.
blocks.correct: A fruit develops from the ovary (two scars); a seed develops from an ovule (one scar).

blocks.q: Give two benefits of improving seed varieties.
blocks.a: They give higher yields and resist drought (also resist disease, or mature early).

items: The ovary becomes a fruit and ovules become seeds.
A fruit has two scars and is juicy; a seed has one scar and is dry.
Seeds propagate the next generation and prevent extinction.
Improved seeds give higher yields, resist drought and disease, and mature early.
Describe what seed dispersal is.
Describe the ways in which seeds are dispersed.
Explain the importance of seed dispersal.

lines: How do seeds travel far from the parent plant?
Why is it bad for all seeds to fall in one place?

rows.def: Moving seeds away from the parent plant.
Seeds carried by wind (light, often winged).
Pods bursting open to throw out their seeds.

blocks.text: **Seed dispersal** is moving seeds away from the parent plant. This avoids overcrowding and competition for light, water and nutrients, and helps plants spread to new areas. Seeds are only dispersed when mature.
Ways of seed dispersal

cells: Seeds small and light; some have wings (jacaranda) or tiny hairs (dandelion).
Seeds light, with air spaces so they float; e.g. coconut, water lily.
Seeds with hooks (black jack) catch on fur; or animals eat fruits (guava) and drop seeds far away.
Pods burst open and throw out seeds; e.g. peas, beans, balsam.

blocks.caption: A pod bursting open (self-dispersal)
lines: A dry seed pod has lines of weakness along its sides.
Heat from the sun (or being stepped on) makes the pod split suddenly.
The seeds are flung out, away from the parent plant.
For each method, picture one example: wind–jacaranda, water–coconut, animal–black jack, explosion–beans.

blocks.q: How are jacaranda seeds dispersed?
blocks.a: By wind.

blocks.q: Give one seed dispersed by explosion.
blocks.a: Beans (or peas, balsam).

blocks.wrong: Coconuts are dispersed by wind.
blocks.correct: Coconuts float, so they are dispersed by water.

blocks.q: State two ways seeds are dispersed by animals.
blocks.a: Seeds with hooks attach to animal fur; and animals eat fruits and drop the seeds far away.

items: Seed dispersal moves mature seeds away from the parent plant.
Methods: wind (light, winged), water (floating), animals (hooks or eaten), self/explosive (bursting pods).
Dispersal reduces competition and spreads plants to new areas.
Explain what plant propagation is.
Describe propagation by seeds.
Describe methods of vegetative propagation.

lines: Have you seen a small banana plant growing beside a big one?
Can a new plant grow without a seed?

rows.def: Producing new plants.
Growing new plants from parts of a plant.
A piece of stem, leaf or root used to grow a new plant.
Joining a twig (scion) onto another plant.
A small plant growing beside the parent.

blocks.text: **Plant propagation** is the production of new plants. It can be done from seeds or from parts of existing plants (vegetative propagation).
Propagation by seeds

items: **Row planting** — seeds planted in rows, e.g. maize, beans.
**Broadcasting** — seeds scattered over the field, then covered, e.g. millet, sorghum.

blocks.text: Vegetative propagation

items: **Cuttings** — grow a new plant from a stem, leaf or root, e.g. cassava, sugarcane, sweet potatoes.
**Grafting** — join a twig (scion) onto the stem (stock) of another plant, e.g. oranges, mangoes.
**Layering (marcotting)** — peg a branch into the soil to grow roots, then cut it off, e.g. passion fruit.
**Budding** — a form of grafting where a bud is joined to a rootstock.
**Suckers** — small plantlets beside the parent (e.g. banana) are separated and replanted.

lines: Grafting joins a scion onto a stock so the two grow as one plant. It improves the quality and yield of a plant that was formerly poor.
Link each method to a plant: cuttings–cassava, grafting–mango, suckers–banana, broadcasting–millet, row planting–maize.

blocks.q: How is cassava usually grown?
blocks.a: From stem cuttings.

blocks.q: What is the small plant growing beside a banana called?
blocks.a: A sucker.

blocks.wrong: All plants can only grow from seeds.
blocks.correct: Many plants grow vegetatively from cuttings, suckers, grafting or layering.

blocks.q: Name two methods of vegetative propagation.
blocks.a: Cuttings and grafting (also layering, budding, suckers).

items: Propagation is the production of new plants — from seeds or vegetatively.
Seed methods: row planting (maize, beans) and broadcasting (millet, sorghum).
Vegetative methods: cuttings, grafting, layering, budding and suckers.
Explain what energy is and identify different types.
Explain how energy is converted from one form to another.

lines: What gives you the energy to run and play?
Can energy ever disappear completely?

rows.def: The ability to do work.
The energy of a moving object.
Stored energy due to position or state.
Changing energy from one form to another.

blocks.text: **Energy** is the ability to do work. We use energy when we walk, lift, dig, sweep or wash.
Types of energy

cells: Energy due to motion and position; sum of kinetic and potential energy.
Energy in a moving object (wind, running water).
Stored energy due to position or state (a stretched rubber band).
From batteries, generators/dynamos and solar panels.
Stored in fuels, food and batteries; released when burnt or used.
Energy related to temperature; main source is the Sun.
Lets us see. Natural: Sun, fireflies. Artificial: torch, bulb.

blocks.text: Energy conversion
**Energy conversion** is changing energy from one form to another. The **law of conservation of energy** says energy cannot be created or destroyed — only changed from one form to another.

items: Torch: chemical → electrical → heat → light.
Burning wood: chemical → heat → light.
Striking a match: chemical → mechanical → heat and light.

lines: Practise writing energy-change chains with arrows. Many questions show a device and ask for the energy change — the torch and the bulb are favourites.

blocks.q: What is energy?
blocks.a: The ability to do work.

blocks.q: Energy in a moving object is called?
blocks.a: Kinetic energy.

blocks.wrong: Energy can be created and destroyed.
blocks.correct: Energy cannot be created or destroyed; it only changes from one form to another.

blocks.q: State the energy changes that happen in a torch.
blocks.a: Chemical to electrical to heat to light.

items: Energy is the ability to do work.
Types include kinetic, potential, chemical, electrical, sound, heat and light.
Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only converted from one form to another.
Explain what an electric current does.
Construct a simple electric circuit.
Distinguish between series and parallel circuits.
Describe the action of a switch.

lines: What makes a bulb light up in a torch?
Why do some string lights all go off when one bulb breaks?

rows.def: The flow of charge (electrons) in a circuit.
The path that lets the current flow.
One path; bulbs share voltage and all go off if one is removed.
Separate paths; bulbs stay bright and work independently.
A material that lets electricity pass through.

blocks.text: An **electric current** is the flow of charge (electrons) from the positive to the negative terminal of a cell. An **electric circuit** is the pathway (usually a wire) that lets the current flow. For current to flow, the circuit must be complete.
Parts of a simple circuit

items: **Dry cell / battery** — the source of electrical energy (stores chemical energy).
**Conductor / wire** — allows the current to flow.
**Converter** — changes electrical energy into another form, e.g. a bulb (to heat and light).
**Switch** — opens (breaks) or closes (completes) the circuit.

blocks.text: Series and parallel circuits

cells: Bulbs are in one line, one switch.
Bulbs are in separate lines, each can have its own switch.
If one bulb is removed, the others go off.
If one bulb goes off, the others keep lighting.
Bulbs share the voltage — they light dimly.
Bulbs do not share voltage — they light fully.
Cells in series: voltages add up, do not last long.
Cells in parallel: voltage like one cell, last longer.

lines: A switch controls the current. Open switch = gap = incomplete circuit = bulb off. Closed switch = complete circuit = bulb on.
Series = one path, dim, all go off together. Parallel = many paths, bright, independent. Practise drawing both and predicting what happens if one bulb is removed.

blocks.q: What must a circuit be for current to flow?
blocks.a: Complete (closed).

blocks.q: In which circuit do the other bulbs stay on if one is removed?
blocks.a: Parallel.

blocks.wrong: In a series circuit the others stay on if one bulb is removed.
blocks.correct: In series, removing one bulb breaks the circuit and all go off; in parallel they stay on.

blocks.q: Explain one advantage of a parallel circuit over a series circuit.
blocks.a: In parallel, each bulb lights at full brightness and the others keep working if one fails.

items: Current is the flow of charge from positive to negative; the circuit must be complete.
A simple circuit has a cell, wires, a converter (bulb) and a switch.
Series: share voltage, dim, all go off if one is removed. Parallel: full brightness, others stay on.

blocks.quizTitle: 5.2 Electric Current and Circuits — Practice Quiz

items: Identify the causes of lightning.
Explain its effects on people, plants, animals and buildings.
Demonstrate how to prevent damage from lightning.
State the importance of lightning in farming.

lines: Have you ever seen lightning during a thunderstorm?
What do you think causes it?

rows.def: A flash of bright light caused by electricity in the clouds.
Electric charge that builds up on objects or in clouds.
A metal (copper) rod that protects buildings from lightning.
Substances formed when lightning fixes nitrogen; plants use them to make proteins.

blocks.text: **Lightning** is a flash of bright light caused by an electric spark — a sudden, powerful flow of electricity between charged clouds during thunderstorms. It is caused by **static electricity** that builds up as rain falls through the air.
Effects of lightning

items: **People** — burns, heart and breathing problems, paralysis, even death.
**Plants** — strikes the tallest trees; a major cause of forest fires.
**Animals** — direct strikes or ground currents can kill many at once; thunder can damage eardrums.
**Buildings** — structural damage, fires, and damage to electronics.

blocks.text: Preventing damage
Tall buildings are fitted with a **lightning arrester** (Franklin's rod) — a thick **copper** wire with a pointed metal rod that carries the lightning charge safely to the ground.

items: Do not lean on walls or stand in water.
Wear thick rubber-soled shoes.
Avoid sharp metal objects.
Do not shelter under trees or walk in open fields; do not swim.
Indoors, stay away from windows and appliances and do not use the phone.

lines: Importance in farming: a lightning flash breaks nitrogen in the air into nitrates, which are washed into the soil. Plants use nitrates to make proteins.
Two things examiners love: a lightning arrester is made of thick COPPER, and lightning's farming benefit is fixing NITROGEN into the soil as nitrates.

blocks.caption: A lightning strike and a lightning arrester
lines: Cloud with negative charges at the bottom
Positive charges on the ground below
The bright lightning flash between cloud and ground
A lightning arrester (pointed copper rod) on the building
The earth wire running down to the ground

blocks.q: What causes lightning?
blocks.a: Lightning is caused by static electricity that builds up in clouds during thunderstorms.

blocks.q: Why are lightning arresters made of copper?
blocks.a: Copper is a very good conductor, so it carries the charge safely to the ground.

blocks.wrong: Lightning is caused by fire in the sky.
blocks.correct: Lightning is caused by static electricity.

blocks.q: State one importance of lightning in farming.
blocks.a: Lightning helps to change nitrogen in the air into nitrates, which plants use to make proteins.

items: Lightning is caused by static electricity in clouds and strikes the tallest objects.
It can harm people, plants, animals and buildings.
A lightning arrester (thick copper wire) carries the charge safely to the ground.
Lightning fixes nitrogen into the soil as nitrates, which plants use to make proteins.
Describe the solar system and the difference between the Sun and its planets.
State the reasons for seasons and day & night.
Compare the movements of the Earth and the Moon.
Describe solar and lunar eclipses, and the uses of solar energy.

lines: Why do we have day and night?
Why is it hotter in some months than in others?

blocks.text: The **solar system** is the **Sun** at the centre, the eight planets and their moons, plus comets, asteroids and constellations. The Sun is a **star** — it produces its own light and heat (it is **luminous**). Planets are **non-luminous**: they only reflect light from the Sun.
The eight planets (in order from the Sun)

cells: Closest to the Sun; smallest; no moon; shortest orbit.
Third from the Sun; only planet known to support life; has 1 moon.
Fifth planet; the largest; many moons.
Sixth; second largest; has rings of rock and ice.
Eighth and farthest; coldest; longest orbit.

blocks.text: Day & night and the seasons

items: **Day and night** are caused by the Earth spinning (rotating) on its own axis every 24 hours.
**Seasons** are caused by the **tilt** of the Earth's axis as it revolves around the Sun — NOT by distance from the Sun.

lines: The Earth takes about 365¼ days (one year) to revolve around the Sun; the extra ¼ day gives a leap year every four years. The Moon is a natural satellite of the Earth and takes about 28 days to orbit it.

blocks.text: Eclipses

cells: The Moon comes between the Sun and the Earth and blocks the Sun's light. Happens only at new moon.
The Earth comes between the Sun and the Moon; the Earth's shadow falls on the Moon. Happens only at full moon.

rows.def: The dark, total shadow formed during a total eclipse.
The lighter, partial shadow formed during a partial eclipse.

lines: Remember: day/night = rotation (spinning), seasons = tilt. Solar eclipse = Moon in the middle; lunar eclipse = Earth in the middle. Umbra = total (dark), penumbra = partial.

blocks.q: What causes day and night?
blocks.a: The Earth spinning (rotating) on its axis.

blocks.q: What causes the seasons?
blocks.a: The tilt of the Earth's axis as it orbits the Sun.

blocks.wrong: Seasons are caused by the Earth's distance from the Sun.
blocks.correct: Seasons are caused by the tilt of the Earth's axis.

blocks.q: Explain the difference between a solar and a lunar eclipse.
blocks.a: A solar eclipse happens when the Moon comes between the Sun and the Earth. A lunar eclipse happens when the Earth comes between the Sun and the Moon.

items: The Sun (a luminous star) is at the centre; the planets are non-luminous and reflect its light.
Day and night come from rotation; seasons come from the tilt of the axis.
Earth orbits the Sun in ~365¼ days; the Moon orbits Earth in ~28 days.
Solar eclipse: Moon between Sun and Earth. Lunar eclipse: Earth between Sun and Moon.
Give examples and uses of common metals and non-metals.
Explain alloys, conductors and insulators.

lines: Why are cooking pots made of metal but their handles of plastic?
Which materials let electricity pass through them?

blocks.text: Substances can be classified as **metals** or **non-metals**, and their properties decide how they are used. Most metals conduct electricity and heat (good conductors); most non-metals do not (insulators).
Common metals and their uses

cells: Light but strong: aeroplane bodies, power lines, pots, foil.
Excellent conductor; does not rust easily: cables, coins, water pipes.
Strong: tools, nails, roofing sheets; alloyed with carbon to make steel.
Coats (galvanises) iron to stop rust; battery cases.
Best conductor of electricity; cutlery, jewellery.
Conducts well, does not tarnish: jewellery, electrical parts.
The only metal that is liquid at room temperature: thermometers.

blocks.text: Non-metals and their uses

items: **Wood** — strong, light; tool handles, fuel, building.
**Glass** — transparent, poor conductor; window panes, insulators.
**Plastic & rubber** — light, do not rust or conduct; insulators on handles and wires.

rows.def: A mixture of two or more metals; e.g. brass (copper + zinc), bronze (copper + tin).
A material that lets electricity (or heat) pass through it — most metals.
A material that does not let electricity pass through it — most non-metals.

lines: Learn the odd ones out: mercury is the only liquid metal, silver is the best conductor, and brass/bronze are the common alloys.

blocks.q: Which metal is liquid at room temperature?
blocks.a: Mercury.

blocks.q: Brass is an alloy of which two metals?
blocks.a: Copper and zinc.

blocks.wrong: All non-metals conduct electricity.
blocks.correct: Most non-metals are insulators; metals are the good conductors.

blocks.q: Define an alloy and give one example.
blocks.a: An alloy is a mixture of two or more metals, for example brass (copper + zinc).

items: Metals are good conductors; non-metals are insulators.
Mercury is the only liquid metal; silver is the best conductor of electricity.
An alloy is a mixture of metals: brass = copper + zinc, bronze = copper + tin.
Identify minerals mined in Zambia and the properties of copper.
Explain how copper is extracted and refined.
Describe the impact of mining on the environment.

lines: Which minerals have you heard are mined in Zambia?
Why is copper so important to our country?

rows.def: Getting minerals (ores) from underground.
A rock containing minerals mixed with impurities.
Heating copper concentrate to melt and separate the copper.
Using electricity to refine copper to 100% purity.
Removing the last impurities to get pure metal.

blocks.text: **Mining** is the process of getting mineral **ores** from underground. An ore is a rock that contains minerals mixed with impurities. Deep ores are mined by the **shaft** method; ores near the surface by the **open-cast** method.
Minerals mined in Zambia

blocks.caption: Map of Zambia showing where minerals are mined

blocks.text: Copper (the most important, mined on the Copperbelt and at Kansanshi and Lumwana in Solwezi), cobalt, iron, gold, lead, zinc, tin, coal (at Maamba), manganese and uranium. **Copper is Zambia's highest export earner.**
Properties of copper

items: A very good conductor of electricity and heat.
Resists corrosion — does not rust easily (used in water pipes).
Malleable — can be hammered into shape.
Ductile — can be stretched into wires.
Shiny; mixes easily to form alloys (brass, bronze).

blocks.text: Extraction of copper (three stages)

items: Crushing — the ore is broken up and crushed into powder in a ball mill.
Floatation — the powder is mixed with water and oil; air is blown through and copper floats (about 96% pure).
Smelting — the concentrate is heated in a furnace to melt and separate the copper (about 98% pure).

blocks.text: **Refining** is the last stage — removing the remaining impurities to get 100% pure copper, using **electrolysis**. The impure copper is the anode and pure copper the cathode; pure copper builds up on the cathode and impurities sink as sludge.

Making copper items in Zambia creates jobs, brings foreign exchange and raises government revenue. But mining harms the environment: it destroys the landscape, clears vegetation and habitats, pollutes air, land and water (acid rain), and uses huge amounts of electricity and water.

lines: Memorise copper extraction in three steps — Crushing, Floatation, Smelting — then Refining by electrolysis. Remember copper is Zambia's top export earner.

blocks.q: What are the three stages of copper extraction?
blocks.a: Crushing, floatation and smelting.

blocks.q: Which process gives 100% pure copper?
blocks.a: Refining by electrolysis.

blocks.wrong: Diamond is one of the main minerals mined in Zambia.
blocks.correct: Diamond is not mined in Zambia; copper is the main mineral.

blocks.q: State two ways mining harms the environment.
blocks.a: It pollutes air, land and water, and it destroys vegetation and animal habitats.

items: Mining gets mineral ores from underground (shaft or open-cast methods).
Copper is the most mined mineral and Zambia's highest export earner.
Copper extraction: crushing, floatation, smelting; refined by electrolysis.
Mining brings jobs and revenue but pollutes air, land and water and destroys habitats.
Describe democracy and mention its characteristics.
Explain how democracy protects human rights.
Describe ways women and girls are not treated equally.
Explain forms of child abuse and child labour.

lines: Who chooses the leaders of your country?
What do you think would happen if people could not choose their leaders?

rows.def: Rule by the people.
When people vote to choose their leaders.
The paper on which a voter makes a secret mark.
Treating people unequally and unfairly.
The basic rights every person should have.

blocks.text: **Democracy** means 'rule by the people'. In a democracy the people take part in government by voting in elections, finding out about and discussing issues, attending community meetings and paying taxes.
Elections

Elections are held every few years so people can change leaders who are not serving them well. To vote in Zambia you must be **18 or older, a Zambian citizen with a green National Registration Card, and a registered voter**. Voting is by **secret ballot** placed in a locked ballot box, so elections are free and fair.
Political parties and human rights

items: Members of parliament form groups called **parties**; the party with the most votes forms the government.
A party with fewer votes is a **minority party** or the **opposition**, whose different ideas must still be heard.
A democracy protects human rights. The **United Nations** proclaimed the Universal Declaration of Human Rights on 10 December 1948.
Rights include voting, free movement, free speech, freedom of religion, owning property and being treated equally by the law.

blocks.text: Discrimination, child abuse and child labour

items: **Discrimination** is unequal treatment — e.g. unequal pay, or girls not being sent to school.
Zambia has two laws: **statutory law** (written, passed by government) and **customary law** (followed by tradition, often unwritten).
**Child abuse** includes neglect, beating, rape and human trafficking.
**Child labour** is forcing children to work instead of going to school.

blocks.q: What does the word 'democracy' mean?
blocks.a: Rule by the people.

blocks.q: Name the three things you must be to vote in Zambia.
blocks.a: 18 or older, a Zambian citizen with a green NRC, and a registered voter.

blocks.wrong: In a democracy only the ruling party is allowed to speak.
blocks.correct: The opposition (minority parties) must also be heard — democracy protects the right to different opinions.

blocks.q: State three ways people take part in a democracy.
blocks.a: By voting in elections, discussing and finding out about issues, attending community meetings and paying taxes (any three).

items: Democracy is rule by the people, through free and fair elections.
Human rights are protected; the UN declared the Universal Declaration of Human Rights in 1948.
Discrimination, child abuse and child labour are wrong and against children's rights.
Identify the three organs (branches) of government.
Explain the functions of the legislature, executive and judiciary.
Explain why the branches must stay separate.

lines: Who makes the laws, and who makes sure people obey them?
Should one person be allowed to do all three?

rows.def: The branch that makes laws (Parliament).
The branch that carries out laws.
The branch of courts that enforces laws.
Asking a higher court to hear a case again.
A government worker, e.g. a teacher, nurse or police officer.

blocks.text: Government has **three branches**, like a tree. Each branch does a different job and is separate from the others.

Each branch is **separate** and does not take orders from another. Only the president can choose cabinet ministers; Parliament and the president cannot tell the courts what to do. This **separation of powers** stops any one branch from having too much power.
The courts
There are four levels of courts in Zambia. If you lose a case you can **appeal** to a higher court. The **Supreme Court** is the highest court and its decisions are final.

blocks.q: Name the three branches of government and what each does.
blocks.a: Legislature (makes laws), Executive (carries out laws), Judiciary (enforces laws).

blocks.q: Which is the highest court in Zambia?
blocks.a: The Supreme Court.

blocks.wrong: The president can tell the courts what to decide.
blocks.correct: The branches are separate — the president and Parliament cannot tell the courts what to do.

blocks.q: Why is it important that the three branches of government stay separate?
blocks.a: So that no single branch gets too much power, and each can check that the others do their job well.

items: Government has three branches: legislature, executive and judiciary.
They are separate (separation of powers) so no branch has too much power.
The Supreme Court is the highest court; you can appeal to a higher court.
Explain the work of the executive branch.
Explain how government serves the people.
Explain how government is paid for (taxes and revenue).

lines: Who builds the schools, hospitals and roads in your area?
Where does the government get the money to do this?

rows.def: The head of government.
A leader chosen by the president to run a ministry.
The people employed to do government work.
Money the government collects, mainly from taxes.

blocks.text: The **executive** carries out the laws and runs the country. At the top are the **president** (head of government), the **vice-president** (who stands in for the president), and the **cabinet ministers** the president chooses.

Government provides services such as building schools and hospitals. It employs **civil servants** — teachers, police officers, doctors and nurses — to do this work. Their salaries are paid from **taxes**.
Where the money comes from

items: Government collects **taxes** from citizens and businesses.
This money is called **revenue**.
The **Ministry of Finance** decides how to spend the revenue, but the National Assembly must approve the plan.

blocks.q: What is the money the government collects from taxes called?
blocks.a: Revenue.

blocks.q: Name two civil servants paid from taxes.
blocks.a: Any two: teachers, police officers, doctors, nurses.

blocks.wrong: The government's money comes from the president's own pocket.
blocks.correct: Government money (revenue) comes mainly from taxes paid by citizens and businesses.

blocks.q: How does the government get the money to pay civil servants?
blocks.a: It collects taxes from citizens and businesses; this revenue is used to pay salaries and provide services.

items: The executive (president, vice-president, ministers) carries out laws and runs services.
Civil servants such as teachers and nurses do government work.
Government is funded by taxes (revenue); the Ministry of Finance plans the spending.
Explain what a ministry is.
Identify the main government ministries and what each does.

lines: If a road is broken, which part of government should fix it?
Why is government divided into different ministries?

rows.def: A national department run by a cabinet minister.
Collects taxes and decides how to spend them.
Keeps records of people and issues passports and NRCs.

blocks.text: The president chooses **cabinet ministers**, who are part of the executive. Each minister runs a different national department called a **ministry**.
Some important ministries

cells: Manages education from pre-school to university.
Supplies medicines, doctors, nurses, hospitals and clinics.
Records of citizens; issues NRCs, birth/death records and passports; prisons.
Controls the army and air force; keeps people safe from outside danger.
Collects taxes and decides how to spend them.
Looks after roads, public buildings, phones and the internet.

blocks.q: Which ministry issues passports and national registration cards?
blocks.a: The Ministry of Home Affairs.

blocks.q: Which ministry looks after the food supply?
blocks.a: The Ministry of Agriculture and Livestock.

blocks.wrong: The Ministry of Health controls the army.
blocks.correct: The Ministry of Defence controls the army and air force; the Ministry of Health looks after medicines, hospitals and clinics.

blocks.q: Name the ministry responsible for collecting taxes, and one responsible for roads.
blocks.a: Finance collects taxes; Transport, Works, Supply and Communications looks after roads.

items: A ministry is a national department run by a cabinet minister.
Key ministries: Education, Health, Home Affairs, Defence, Finance, Agriculture, Transport.
Ministries help the president put laws into action and serve the people.
State what a constitution is and why it is important.
State the main things the Zambian constitution says.
Name the types of elections and how the constitution is changed.

lines: What are the most important rules for your country?
Who should the rules apply to — only ordinary people, or the leaders too?

rows.def: The set of written rules for a country; the supreme law.
A vote where citizens say Yes or No to a change.
An election to replace a leader who dies, steps down or loses their job.

blocks.text: A **constitution** is a set of written rules for a country. It tells the people in government how to rule, and it is the **most important law in the land**.
What Zambia's constitution says

items: Zambia is a Christian country, but everyone is free to choose their own religion.
The people must choose their government leaders.
The government must protect human rights and answer to the citizens.
Men and women must be treated equally.
The government must be honest and help improve people's lives.

blocks.text: Elections and changing the constitution

items: **General elections** every five years elect the president and members of parliament.
**Local government elections** elect councillors.
**By-elections** replace a leader who dies, steps down or loses their job.
To change a law, **two-thirds of parliament** must agree. If a change affects citizens' rights, there must be a **referendum**.

blocks.q: What is a constitution?
blocks.a: The set of written rules for a country; it is the most important (supreme) law.

blocks.q: How often are general elections held in Zambia?
blocks.a: Every five years.

blocks.wrong: The constitution can be changed easily by one minister.
blocks.correct: Changing it needs two-thirds of parliament to agree, and a referendum if citizens' rights are affected.

blocks.q: Name two ways the constitution of Zambia can be changed.
blocks.a: A two-thirds vote of members of parliament, and a referendum where citizens vote Yes or No (when rights are affected).

items: A constitution is the supreme written law of a country.
Zambia's constitution is democratic: it protects rights and equality and makes government accountable.
Elections: general, local government and by-elections. Changing it needs two-thirds of parliament (and a referendum for rights).
Name the seven continents.
Compare the continents by size and population.
Locate the continents on a world map.

lines: How many continents can you name without looking?
Which one do you live on?

rows.def: One of the seven huge landmasses of the world.
A very large area of land.
The number of people living in a place.
An imaginary line around the middle of the Earth.

blocks.text: The world has **seven continents**: Africa, Asia, Europe, North America, South America, Antarctica and Australia.

cells: The largest continent (about 30% of land) and the most people (about 4.3 billion).
The second largest; home to famous wildlife.
About 17% of the world's land; joined to South America by a narrow strip.
The equator passes through it; has the Andes, the world's longest mountain range.
The coldest and least populated; includes the South Pole; covered in ice.
One of the smallest, but densely populated.

blocks.q: Which is the largest continent and which has the most people?
blocks.a: Asia is both the largest and the most populated.

blocks.q: Which continent is the coldest and least populated?
blocks.a: Antarctica.

blocks.wrong: Africa is the largest continent.
blocks.correct: Asia is the largest continent; Africa is the second largest.

blocks.q: Name the seven continents of the world.
blocks.a: Africa, Asia, Europe, North America, South America, Antarctica and Australia.

items: There are seven continents.
Asia is the largest and most populated; Antarctica is the coldest and least populated.
The equator passes through South America and Africa.
Identify the main physical features of the world.
Describe rivers, lakes, mountains, deserts, plains and plateaus.
Give examples of each feature.

lines: What different landforms have you seen — hills, rivers, plains?
How do you think mountains were made?

rows.def: A natural landform such as a mountain, river or desert.
A mountain pushed up when continents shifted.
A large area of high, flat land (a tableland).
A large area of low, flat land, often grassland.

blocks.text: Water features

items: **Rivers** flow across the land. A **lake** is water surrounded by land; **Lake Victoria** is Africa's largest lake.
There are **four oceans**; seas are smaller and often bordered by land (e.g. the Red Sea).
**Islands** vary from tiny atolls to large ones like Madagascar.

blocks.text: Land features

items: **Mountains** are the most common feature; **fold mountains** include the Atlas, Andes, Alps, Rockies and Drakensberg. The **Himalayas** are the highest range.
**Deserts** cover about one-third of the Earth — hot deserts like the Sahara and Kalahari, and cold deserts.
**Valleys and lowlands**: the **Great Rift Valley** runs through East Africa. **Plains** (like the prairies and steppes) are grasslands used for crops.
**Plateaus** are high flat lands; much of southern Africa is the **Great African Plateau**.

blocks.q: What is the highest mountain range in the world?
blocks.a: The Himalayas.

blocks.q: About what fraction of the Earth is desert?
blocks.a: About one-third.

blocks.wrong: A lake and a river are the same thing.
blocks.correct: A river flows; a lake is a body of water surrounded by land and does not flow.

blocks.q: Explain the difference between a river and a lake.
blocks.a: A river flows across the land, while a lake is an area of water surrounded by land that does not flow.

items: Physical features include rivers, lakes, mountains, deserts, plains and plateaus.
The Himalayas are the highest mountains; the Sahara and Kalahari are hot deserts.
The Great Rift Valley and the Great African Plateau are important features of Africa.

blocks.quizTitle: 2.2 Physical Features of the World — Practice Quiz

items: Explain the reasons for rapid population growth.
Describe the effects of rapid population growth.
Suggest solutions.

lines: Is your town more crowded than it was a few years ago?
What problems happen when too many people live in one place?

rows.def: The number of births per thousand people per year.
The number of deaths per thousand people per year.
Too many people for the resources available.
When a country's economy moves from farming to factories.

blocks.text: Why populations grow

items: Better science and technology means more food can be grown.
Better health care means people live longer and fewer children die.
Early marriages and poor family planning lead to large families.
Polygamy (one man with many wives) and improved sanitation also raise the population.

blocks.text: Effects of rapid growth

When a population grows too fast there are **not enough hospitals, schools or jobs**. This leads to disease, unemployment, crime, **slums (shanty compounds)**, food shortages and environmental problems like deforestation and pollution.
Solutions

items: Family planning and education.
Better farming methods to grow more food.
New industries to create jobs.

blocks.q: What is the birth rate?
blocks.a: The number of births per thousand people per year.

blocks.q: Give two effects of rapid population growth.
blocks.a: Any two: not enough schools/hospitals/jobs, slums, crime, food shortages, pollution.

blocks.wrong: Population grows only because more babies are born.
blocks.correct: It also grows because people live longer (lower death rate) thanks to better health care.

blocks.q: Suggest two ways to solve the problems of rapid population growth.
blocks.a: Family planning and education, better farming methods, and creating new industries to provide jobs (any two).

items: Populations grow because of better health care, more food, early marriages and poor family planning.
Rapid growth strains schools, hospitals and jobs and causes slums and food shortages.
Solutions: family planning, education, better farming and new industries.
Explain what corruption is.
Discuss the causes and effects of corruption.
Give examples of corruption.

lines: Have you heard the phrase 'paying under the table'?
Why is it unfair when an official takes a bribe?

rows.def: Dishonest behaviour by people in positions of power.
Money or favours given to make someone act dishonestly.
Taking responsibility for what you say and do.

blocks.text: **Corruption** is when people in positions of power behave dishonestly. Because governments decide how to spend public money, officials have power, so people try to **bribe** them — paying money 'under the table' where no one can see.
Examples of corruption

items: Bribing an official to get an NRC or a driving licence.
Bribing a school principal to accept a child.
Bribing a teacher to leak an exam paper (called **rigging** an exam).
Bribing a police officer to free a criminal.
Bribing officials to win large government contracts.

blocks.q: What is corruption?
blocks.a: Dishonest behaviour by people in positions of power, such as taking bribes.

blocks.q: What is it called when an exam paper is leaked through bribery?
blocks.a: Rigging an exam.

blocks.wrong: Corruption only happens on a very large scale.
blocks.correct: Corruption can be large or small — even a small bribe for a document is corruption.

blocks.q: Give two examples of corruption.
blocks.a: Any two: bribing an official for a document, leaking an exam paper, bribing a police officer, or paying a bribe to win a contract.

items: Corruption is dishonest behaviour by people in power, often through bribes.
It happens on a large or small scale.
It is unfair because it lets some people get what they should not, at the expense of others.
List the causes of food shortages.
Explain food security and malnutrition.
Suggest ways to make sure there is enough food.

lines: What would happen if the rains failed for a whole year?
Why do some countries have to buy food from others?

rows.def: Making sure there is enough food now and in the future.
Illness caused by not getting enough good food.
A crop grown to sell for money rather than to eat.
A long period with little or no rain.

blocks.text: Causes of food shortages

items: Overpopulation — food is produced more slowly than people are born.
Climate change, droughts, floods and other natural disasters.
Deforestation and overgrazing turn farmland into desert.
Soil erosion and poor farming methods.
Wars (people flee and land is unfarmed) and diseases (sick people cannot work).
Growing cash crops instead of food, so food must be imported (which costs more).

blocks.text: Food security

**Food security** means making sure there is enough food for everyone now and in the future. Too little food causes **malnutrition**, so people are weak and cannot work. Governments can help by promoting good farming, supplying seeds and fertilisers, irrigating dry areas, improving storage and creating markets for crops.

blocks.q: What does 'food security' mean?
blocks.a: Making sure there is enough food to feed the people now and in the future.

blocks.q: Name two causes of food shortages.
blocks.a: Any two: overpopulation, drought/floods, deforestation, overgrazing, soil erosion, poor farming, wars, diseases, cash crops.

blocks.wrong: Growing cash crops always increases a country's food supply.
blocks.correct: Cash crops are grown to sell, not to eat, so a country may still have to import food.

blocks.q: State two ways a country can make sure there is enough food for the future.
blocks.a: Use good farming methods and supply seeds/fertilisers; irrigate dry areas; improve storage; ensure a market for crops (any two).

items: Food shortages are caused by overpopulation, climate change, disasters, poor farming, wars and cash crops.
Food security means enough food now and in future; too little causes malnutrition.
Good farming, irrigation, better storage and markets improve food security.
Explain why HIV and AIDS is a global challenge.
Describe how HIV and AIDS affects food security and the economy.

lines: How can illness in a community affect how much food is grown?
Why is HIV and AIDS a challenge for the whole country, not just the sick person?

rows.def: The virus that causes AIDS.
Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome; it weakens the body's defences.
How a country produces, sells and uses goods, services and money.

blocks.text: HIV and AIDS is one of the world's great challenges. It affects not only health but also **food security and the economy**.
How it affects food and the economy

items: People who are sick with AIDS **cannot work as hard**, so less food is produced.
Families may leave their small farms when members fall ill, threatening food security.
Money and time are spent caring for the sick instead of on development.
The workplace loses skilled workers, which slows the economy.

blocks.q: How does HIV and AIDS affect food production?
blocks.a: Sick people cannot work as hard, so less food is grown; families may leave their farms.

blocks.wrong: HIV and AIDS only affects the person who is sick.
blocks.correct: It affects the whole community — food production, families and the economy all suffer.

blocks.q: Explain how HIV and AIDS can reduce a country's food supply.
blocks.a: People who are sick cannot farm properly, families may abandon small farms, and there are fewer healthy workers, so less food is produced.

items: HIV and AIDS is a global challenge that goes beyond health.
It reduces food production because sick people cannot work.
It harms the economy by removing healthy, skilled workers.
List the different forms of pollution.
Discuss the effects of pollution.
Suggest solutions to pollution.

lines: What rubbish or smoke do you see around your area?
What harm do you think it does?

rows.def: Harmful substances that make the environment dirty and unhealthy.
Rain made acidic by gases from factories and vehicles.
Human body waste.
Coal, oil and natural gas that formed over millions of years.

blocks.text: Forms of pollution

items: **Air pollution** — burning fossil fuels in power stations, factories and cars.
**Soil pollution** — rubbish, human waste, pesticides and soil erosion.
**Noise pollution** — vehicles, machinery, aircraft and loud music.
**Water pollution** — factory waste, sewage, oil spills and fertilisers (which make algae use up the oxygen so fish die).

blocks.text: Effects

Air pollution damages lungs and eyes and causes **acid rain**, which kills trees and eats into rocks and buildings. Water pollution causes diseases like cholera, and harms fish and seabirds. Pollution also causes **global warming**.

blocks.q: Name the four main forms of pollution.
blocks.a: Air, soil (land), noise and water pollution.

blocks.q: What causes acid rain?
blocks.a: Gases from factories and vehicles that mix with moisture in the air.

blocks.wrong: Only factories cause pollution.
blocks.correct: Pollution also comes from cars, homes (cleaning chemicals), farms (pesticides) and noise.

blocks.q: List three ways we can reduce pollution from cars.
blocks.a: Use public transport, walk or cycle, keep engines well serviced, and use cleaner fuels (any three).

items: Pollution can be of air, soil, noise or water.
It harms health, causes acid rain and global warming, and kills wildlife.
We reduce it by burning fewer fossil fuels, managing waste and using cleaner methods.
Identify other world challenges such as war, global warming and poverty.
Suggest solutions to these challenges.

lines: Why do people sometimes have to leave their homes and country?
What does it mean to be poor?

rows.def: A person who flees their home, usually because of war.
The warming of the Earth caused by greenhouse gases.
Not having enough money for food, clothes, health care and schooling.
Unable to read or write.

blocks.text: War and refugees

War forces people to leave their homes; these people are called **refugees** and often live in camps with too little food, water or schools. The **United Nations (UN)** and the **African Union (AU)** help fighting groups make peace; the International Court of Justice also helps solve conflicts.
Global warming
Burning **fossil fuels** releases greenhouse gases that trap the Sun's heat and make the Earth warmer. This melts ice caps, raises sea levels, causes droughts and disturbs natural habitats.
Poverty

**Poverty** is not having enough money for basic needs. Poor people often cannot afford education, so they remain **illiterate** and cannot get well-paid jobs. **Education** is the key to ending poverty, which is why Zambia provides basic education for all children.

blocks.q: What is a refugee?
blocks.a: A person who flees their home, usually because of war.

blocks.q: What is the main key to ending poverty?
blocks.a: Education.

blocks.wrong: Global warming is caused by the Earth moving closer to the Sun.
blocks.correct: Global warming is caused by greenhouse gases from burning fossil fuels trapping the Sun's heat.

blocks.q: Explain how global warming can affect cities near the sea.
blocks.a: Global warming melts the ice caps, raising sea levels, which can flood low-lying cities near the sea.

items: Other challenges include war (refugees), global warming and poverty.
The UN and AU help solve conflicts.
Global warming comes from greenhouse gases; education helps end poverty.
Explain the importance of family life in Zambia.
Discuss different types of families.
Explain how family life provides security, love and care.

lines: Who looks after you and teaches you right from wrong?
What does the saying 'It takes a village to raise a child' mean?

rows.def: A group of people related by blood, marriage or adoption.
A mother, father and their children.
Parents, children and other relatives such as grandparents, uncles and aunts.
A child whose parents have died.

blocks.text: Families are the **building blocks of society**. It is within our family that we first learn the values and traditions of our culture. Our parents feed, clothe and care for us until we can look after ourselves.
Why family life is important

items: Children receive security, protection, love and care.
Family members support each other; the weak, sick, old and orphaned are cared for.
Children are taught religious beliefs and moral values.
Children raised in families tend to be healthier and have better relationships as adults.

blocks.text: Types of families

items: **Nuclear family** — parents and their children.
**Extended family** — also includes grandparents, uncles, aunts and cousins.
**Single-parent family** — one parent (through death, divorce or never marrying).
**Orphans** are cared for by grandparents or older siblings; **adopted children** are raised by people who are not their birth parents.

blocks.q: What is a nuclear family?
blocks.a: A mother, father and their children.

blocks.q: Complete the saying: 'It takes a ___ to raise a ___.'
blocks.a: It takes a village to raise a child.

blocks.wrong: An extended family is just a mother and father.
blocks.correct: That is a nuclear family. An extended family also includes grandparents, uncles, aunts and cousins.

blocks.q: List three reasons why family life is important.
blocks.a: It gives children security, love and care; family members support each other and care for the weak; and children are taught values (any three).

items: Families are the building blocks of society and teach us values.
Family life gives security, love, care and support.
Types include nuclear, extended, single-parent and families with orphans or adopted children.
Describe the relationship between parents and children in a Zambian family.
Explain matrilineal and patrilineal family systems.
Explain the duties of children towards their parents.

lines: How do you show respect to your parents and elders?
What do your parents teach you that you will need as an adult?

rows.def: A family system where children belong more to the mother's side (e.g. Tonga, Bemba).
A family system where children belong to the father's side (e.g. Lozi, Ngoni).

blocks.text: In Zambian tradition, **family ties are very strong**. There are two systems of family belonging: in **matrilineal** tribes (such as the Tonga and Bemba) children belong more to the mother's side, while in **patrilineal** tribes (such as the Lozi and Ngoni) children belong to the father's side.
Duties of parents and children

items: Parents love and care for their children and **train them in the skills they will need** in adult life.
Children are expected to **respect and obey** their parents — for example, kneeling to show respect.
Children must care for their parents in old age, bury them when they die and look after their graves, and may name their own children after them.

blocks.q: Name the two family systems and give one tribe for each.
blocks.a: Matrilineal (Tonga or Bemba) and patrilineal (Lozi or Ngoni).

blocks.q: Give one way children show respect to parents in Zambian tradition.
blocks.a: By kneeling when giving something to a parent (or obeying and caring for them).

blocks.wrong: In matrilineal tribes children belong to the father's side.
blocks.correct: In matrilineal tribes children belong to the mother's side; in patrilineal tribes they belong to the father's side.

blocks.q: Explain the difference between matrilineal and patrilineal family systems.
blocks.a: In a matrilineal system children belong more to the mother's side (e.g. Tonga, Bemba); in a patrilineal system they belong to the father's side (e.g. Lozi, Ngoni).

items: Zambian families have strong ties.
Matrilineal tribes trace children through the mother; patrilineal through the father.
Parents train and care for children; children respect, obey and later care for their parents.
Say where the Prairies are and what the word means.
Describe the climate, vegetation and farming of the Prairies.

lines: Imagine flat grassland stretching as far as you can see — where might that be?
Why might such land be good for growing crops?

rows.def: A large area of temperate grassland in North America.
The variety of living things in an area.
A mild climate, not very hot or very cold.

blocks.text: The **Prairies** are temperate grasslands in **North America**. The word 'prairie' means grassland/meadow. They can be wet (rich soil, good for farming), mesic (good drainage and soil) or dry (may suffer drought).
Vegetation and farming

items: Originally tall, mixed and short grass with **few trees**, because regular fires killed the trees.
The soil was so thick with roots that strong **iron (steel) ploughs** had to be invented to break it.
Today vast fields grow maize, soya beans, sorghum, barley, oats, sugar beet, cotton, sunflower and groundnuts.
In 2012 the USA prairies produced about **36% of the world's maize**.
Farming has reduced the natural **biodiversity**, so some land is being conserved.

blocks.q: On which continent are the Prairies?
blocks.a: North America.

blocks.q: Why were steel ploughs invented for the Prairie?
blocks.a: Because the soil was so thickly matted with roots that wooden ploughs could not break it.

blocks.wrong: The Prairies are natural forests.
blocks.correct: The Prairies are grasslands with few trees, because regular fires stopped trees from growing.

blocks.q: Give three examples of crops grown on the Prairies.
blocks.a: Any three: maize, soya beans, sorghum, barley, oats, sugar beet, cotton, sunflower, groundnuts.

items: The Prairies are temperate grasslands in North America.
They had few trees because of fire, and needed steel ploughs to farm.
They grow maize and other crops, but have lost much natural biodiversity.
Say where the Pampas are and what the word means.
Describe the climate and farming of the Pampas.

lines: Where in South America might you find huge grassy plains?
What animals would do well on rich grazing land?

rows.def: Fertile temperate grassland plains in South America.
Farm animals such as cattle and sheep.
A natural area at risk of being destroyed.

blocks.text: The **Pampas** are fertile plains covering large parts of **Argentina, Uruguay and parts of Brazil** — about 750,000 km². The word 'pampas' means **'plain'**. They lie in a **temperate region with regular rainfall**, making them one of the world's richest grazing lands.
Farming and threats

items: At first the Pampas were used for raising **cattle and horses**.
Today the rich soil grows maize, wheat, flax and alfalfa, and high-quality **cattle and sheep** are raised; it is also good for dairy, wine and vegetables.
Threats include pollution from fertilisers and pesticides and **overgrazing**, so little of the original grassland remains — it is an **endangered habitat**.

blocks.q: What does the word 'pampas' mean, and where are they?
blocks.a: 'Plain'; they are in South America (Argentina, Uruguay and parts of Brazil).

blocks.q: What two things threaten the Pampas?
blocks.a: Pollution (fertilisers and pesticides) and overgrazing by livestock.

blocks.wrong: The Pampas are in Africa.
blocks.correct: The Pampas are in South America (Argentina, Uruguay and parts of Brazil).

blocks.q: Why do the Pampas make ideal grazing land?
blocks.a: They lie in a temperate region with regular rainfall and deep, rich soil, which produces lush grass for livestock.

items: The Pampas are fertile temperate grasslands of South America; 'pampas' means plain.
Regular rainfall makes them rich grazing for cattle and sheep, and good for crops.
Pollution and overgrazing have made them an endangered habitat.
Say where the Downs are.
Describe the climate, soil and farming of the Darling Downs.

lines: Australia has famous farming areas — can you name one?
Where would farmers get water in a dry country?

rows.def: A farming region in Queensland, Australia.
The world's largest underground body of water, used for irrigation.
Grassland used for grazing animals.

blocks.text: The **Downs** (the **Darling Downs**) is a farming region in **Queensland, Australia**, on the western slopes of the Great Dividing Range. It has a **humid sub-tropical climate** with rainfall of 600–1000 mm.
Soil, crops and water

items: The soil is **rich and black**, with rolling hills covered by **pastures**.
Crops include vegetables, legumes, cotton, wheat, barley and sorghum.
Livestock includes **beef and dairy cattle, pigs and sheep**.
Irrigation water is pumped from underground — the **Great Artesian Basin**, the largest underground water body in the world.

blocks.q: In which country are the Downs found?
blocks.a: Australia (Queensland).

blocks.q: Where does the irrigation water for the Downs come from?
blocks.a: Underground, from the Great Artesian Basin.

blocks.wrong: The Downs get their irrigation water from a large river.
blocks.correct: The water is pumped from underground — the Great Artesian Basin.

blocks.q: Name two crops and two types of livestock farmed in the Downs.
blocks.a: Crops: any two of vegetables, legumes, cotton, wheat, barley, sorghum. Livestock: beef cattle, dairy cattle, pigs or sheep (any two).

items: The Downs (Darling Downs) is a farming region in Queensland, Australia.
It has humid sub-tropical climate, rich black soil and pastures.
It grows wheat, cotton and sorghum and raises cattle, pigs and sheep, using water from the Great Artesian Basin.
Say where the Steppes are.
Explain why there is little farming on the Steppes.
Describe the natural vegetation.

lines: What happens to land that is far from the sea and high near mountains?
Would you expect much rain there?

rows.def: A dry, cold grassland, mostly in Asia.
The combined landmass of Europe and Asia.

blocks.text: The **Steppes** are **dry, cold grasslands** that usually lie between forests and deserts. They are found mostly in **Mongolia, Siberia, Tibet, China and the USA** (where they are called dry prairies).
Climate and vegetation

items: The air is **dry** because the Steppes are far from the ocean and close to mountains.
There is **little farming** because the soil is poor and the climate is cold; rainfall is only 250–750 mm.
The natural plants are mostly **grasses** — tall grass near the forest, short grass near the desert, and mixed grass in between.

blocks.q: Why is there little farming on the Steppes?
blocks.a: Because the soil is poor and the climate is dry and cold.
