blocks.q: Name two places where Steppes are found.
blocks.a: Any two: Mongolia, Siberia, Tibet, China, the USA.

blocks.wrong: The Steppes are warm, wet grasslands.
blocks.correct: The Steppes are dry, cold grasslands with low rainfall.

blocks.q: Describe the natural vegetation of the Steppes.
blocks.a: Mostly grasses — tall grass near the forest, short grass near the desert, and mixed grasses in between, with some shrubs and flowers.

items: The Steppes are dry, cold grasslands of Eurasia (and dry prairies in the USA).
Little farming happens because the soil is poor and the climate cold and dry.
The vegetation is mainly grass: tall near forests, short near deserts.
Explain what the veld is and name its main types.
Describe the farming done on each type of veld.

lines: What word do we use for grassland in southern Africa?
Which type of land would suit cattle, and which would suit growing crops?

rows.def: Open grassland in southern Africa.
High plateau veld (1500–2100 m) used for commercial farming.
Lower, warmer veld (below 500 m) with a wetter climate.
Veld covered with grass and thorny acacia trees.

blocks.text: The **veld** is the open grassland of **southern Africa**. There are three main types:

cells: Commercial farming: livestock, cereal crops and market gardening.
Timber, fruit, tea, coffee, livestock, game farms and vegetables; many wild animals.

blocks.text: Parts of Zambia are covered by **bushveld/thornveld**, which suits cattle farming.

blocks.q: What are the three main types of veld?
blocks.a: Highveld, lowveld and thornveld.

blocks.q: Which type of veld is best for cattle farming?
blocks.a: The thornveld.

blocks.wrong: The veld is found in North America.
blocks.correct: The veld is the grassland of southern Africa.

blocks.q: Name the three types of veld and one farming activity for each.
blocks.a: Highveld (cereals/livestock), lowveld (fruit/tea/game farming) and thornveld (cattle farming).

items: The veld is southern African grassland.
Highveld is used for cereals and livestock; lowveld for fruit, tea and game; thornveld for cattle.
Parts of Zambia are thornveld, suited to cattle.
Explain the aims of SADC and COMESA.
Explain how working together improves trade.

lines: Why might countries agree to work together?
How could removing border taxes help trade?

rows.def: Southern African Development Community.
Common Market for Eastern and Southern Africa.
A group of countries that remove customs and taxes on goods between them.
Roads, railways and power supplies a country needs.

blocks.text: **SADC** (the Southern African Development Community) is a group of southern African countries that work together on **infrastructure** — roads, railways, power, water and electricity — to improve their economies.

**COMESA** (the Common Market for Eastern and Southern Africa) is a **free trade zone** of 20 countries that **remove customs controls and taxes** on goods moving between them. This lets goods travel freely, making transport quicker and cheaper and improving trade.

blocks.q: What do the letters SADC and COMESA stand for?
blocks.a: SADC = Southern African Development Community; COMESA = Common Market for Eastern and Southern Africa.

blocks.q: What is a free trade zone?
blocks.a: A group of countries that remove customs controls and taxes on goods moving between them.

blocks.wrong: SADC and COMESA make each country trade only by itself.
blocks.correct: They help countries work together — sharing infrastructure and removing trade barriers.

blocks.q: How does belonging to a free trade zone improve trade?
blocks.a: Removing customs and taxes lets goods move freely, so transport is quicker and cheaper and more goods are traded.

items: SADC members cooperate on infrastructure to improve their economies.
COMESA is a free trade zone of 20 countries that removes customs and taxes.
Working together makes trade quicker, cheaper and easier.
Name the five types of transport systems.
Describe road and air transport and their importance.

lines: How do goods get from a farm to a town, or to another country?
Which way of travelling is fastest?

rows.def: A way of moving people and goods (road, air, rail, shipping, pipeline).
The roads, railways and facilities transport needs.

blocks.text: There are **five types of transport systems**: **road, air, rail, shipping and pipelines**.
Road and air transport

items: **Roads** are the basis of all transport systems. Vehicles include trucks, buses, cars, bicycles, ox-carts and farm vehicles. Some rural areas have poor roads, so people use bicycles and ox-carts.
**Air transport** is the **quickest but most expensive**. It uses aeroplanes and airfields. It lets fresh farm produce (e.g. peas or flowers) reach Europe within hours, creating new industries.

blocks.q: Name the five types of transport systems.
blocks.a: Road, air, rail, shipping and pipelines.

blocks.q: Which transport system is the quickest but most expensive?
blocks.a: Air transport.

blocks.wrong: Air transport is the cheapest way to move goods.
blocks.correct: Air transport is the quickest but the most expensive way to move goods.

blocks.q: Explain one benefit of air transport for farmers.
blocks.a: Fresh produce such as flowers or peas can be flown to other parts of the world within hours and sold fresh, creating new markets.

items: The five transport systems are road, air, rail, shipping and pipelines.
Roads are the basis of transport; bicycles and ox-carts help in rural areas.
Air transport is quickest but most expensive, and lets fresh produce be exported.
Identify the main communication systems.
Compare fixed lines, fibre-optic cables and radio.
Explain communication and commerce.

lines: How do you send a message to someone far away?
How is this faster than writing a letter?

rows.def: Copper wires on telephone poles that carry an electrical signal.
Glass or plastic cable that carries light signals; very fast.
Buying and selling goods and services.

blocks.text: Communication systems include **fixed lines, fibre-optic cables and radio**. Electronic communication has largely replaced writing letters because it is much faster.

cells: Copper wires that carry an electrical signal from one device to another.
Glass or plastic cables that carry light signals; faster and carry more data than copper.
Uses microwave towers and satellites to send signals between devices.

blocks.text: These days we use the **internet** for **commerce** — buying and selling goods worldwide (for example buying a book online and paying through an electronic payment system), so buyers and sellers no longer need to be in the same place.

blocks.q: What is the difference between a fixed line and a fibre-optic cable?
blocks.a: A fixed line is copper wire carrying an electrical signal; a fibre-optic cable is glass/plastic carrying light signals, and is faster with more data.

blocks.q: Which communication system uses microwave towers and satellites?
blocks.a: Radio.

blocks.wrong: Fixed lines carry more data faster than fibre-optic cables.
blocks.correct: Fibre-optic cables are faster and carry far more data than copper fixed lines.

blocks.q: Why are fibre-optic cables important for global communication?
blocks.a: They carry large amounts of data as light signals at very high speed, which copper fixed lines cannot do.

items: Communication systems: fixed lines (copper/electrical), fibre-optic cables (glass/light, fast) and radio (towers and satellites).
Electronic communication is faster than letters.
The internet allows commerce between buyers and sellers anywhere.
Identify the main parts of a bicycle.
Explain the function of each part.
List uses of a bicycle.

lines: Do you ride or have you seen a bicycle?
Which part keeps you balanced and steering?

rows.def: The tubular steel body of the bicycle.
Used to steer the bicycle.
The seat of the bicycle (adjustable).
A strong holder for carrying things.

blocks.text: A bicycle is a common form of transport made of many parts.

cells: Tubular steel body; strong enough to carry the rider and load.
Used to steer; keep both hands on them.
The seat; can be adjusted to the rider's size.
A strong holder for carrying goods or passengers.
Holds the bicycle up so it does not fall over.
Turned by the feet to drive the wheels.
Stop the bicycle (e.g. the coaster/pedal brake).

blocks.caption: A bicycle and its main parts
lines: The frame (tubular steel body) in the middle
Handle bars at the front for steering
The saddle (seat) on top
Pedals and chain that drive the rear wheel
Brakes, spokes and wheel rims on the wheels
A luggage rack and stand at the back

blocks.q: What is the frame of a bicycle made of, and what does it do?
blocks.a: Tubular steel; it carries the weight of the rider and load.

blocks.q: What are the handle bars used for?
blocks.a: To steer the bicycle.

blocks.wrong: The saddle is used to steer the bicycle.
blocks.correct: The handle bars steer the bicycle; the saddle is the seat.

blocks.q: Name three parts of a bicycle and the function of each.
blocks.a: Frame (carries the rider), handle bars (steer), saddle (seat), brakes (stop), luggage rack (carry goods) — any three.

items: A bicycle's main parts are the frame, handle bars, saddle, pedals, chain, brakes, spokes, rims, luggage rack and stand.
The frame is tubular steel; handle bars steer; the saddle is the adjustable seat.
Bicycles carry people and goods, especially where roads are poor.
Explain how alcohol and drugs affect road safety.
State the rules a cyclist must obey to be safe.

lines: Why is it dangerous to cycle on a busy road?
What could make a driver fail to see a cyclist at night?

rows.def: A part that shines in light so others can see you.
Using your hand to show what you intend to do.

blocks.text: Roads are getting busier, so there are more accidents. **Alcohol and drugs** slow people's reactions and judgement and cause many accidents, so cyclists and drivers must never use them on the road. Cyclists should also not use **earphones**, because they cannot hear traffic.
Rules of the road for cyclists

items: Ride on the **left-hand side** of the road, with the flow of traffic.
Always observe what is happening around you; do not assume drivers have seen you.
Make sure you can be seen — use **reflectors** and wear **bright colours**, especially at night.
Use **hand signals** to show what you intend to do.
Do not use earphones while cycling.

blocks.q: On which side of the road should a cyclist ride in Zambia?
blocks.a: On the left-hand side, with the flow of traffic.

blocks.q: Why should a cyclist not wear earphones on the road?
blocks.a: Because they cannot hear the traffic around them.

blocks.wrong: It is safe to cycle after drinking a little alcohol.
blocks.correct: Alcohol slows reactions and judgement and causes accidents — never cycle (or drive) after drinking.

blocks.q: Write three rules of the road that a cyclist must obey.
blocks.a: Ride on the left with the traffic; be visible with reflectors and bright colours; use hand signals; do not use earphones; stay alert (any three).

items: Alcohol and drugs cause accidents; cyclists must avoid them and not wear earphones.
Cyclists ride on the left with the traffic and stay alert.
Be visible with reflectors and bright colours, and use hand signals.

the removal of undigested food from the body
the taking in of food through the mouth
the movement of food along the gut

2.1 Diseases.q: Viruses and bacteria can only be seen using a
2.1 Diseases.explanation: Both are tiny and can only be seen with a powerful microscope.

2.1 Diseases.q: Which statement about viruses is correct?
options: They can live on their own
Most viral diseases have no cure
2.1 Diseases.explanation: Most viral diseases have no cure; viruses are non-living and cannot live on their own.

2.1 Diseases.q: Which disease is caused by a virus?
2.1 Diseases.explanation: Measles is a viral disease; the others listed are bacterial.

2.1 Diseases.q: Which disease is caused by bacteria?

2.1 Diseases.explanation: Cholera is a waterborne disease spread by drinking contaminated water.

2.1 Diseases.q: Which of the following is a waterborne disease?
2.1 Diseases.explanation: Typhoid is carried in contaminated water and food.
TB attacks and damages the lungs.
AIDS is caused by the Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV).

2.1 Diseases.q: Which is NOT a way HIV is transmitted?
2.1 Diseases.explanation: HIV is not spread by ordinary contact such as shaking hands.

options: Acquired Infection of the Digestive System

2.1 Diseases.q: Drugs that help to control HIV and AIDS are called

2.1 Diseases.q: Scurvy is a disease caused by lack of

Ringworm is a fungal skin disease spread by contact.

2.1 Diseases.q: Which of the following is a disease of the skin?
2.1 Diseases.explanation: Scabies is a contagious skin disease caused by mites.

2.1 Diseases.q: Most viral diseases are best prevented by
2.1 Diseases.explanation: Most viral diseases cannot be cured but can be prevented by immunisation.

2.2 Fruits.explanation: After fertilisation, the ovary enlarges to become a fruit.

2.2 Fruits.q: An indigenous fruit is one that
2.2 Fruits.explanation: Indigenous fruits grow naturally in the wild.

2.2 Fruits.q: Which of the following is an indigenous fruit?
2.2 Fruits.explanation: Snot apple (manego) is an indigenous fruit; the others are exotic.

2.2 Fruits.q: Which of the following is an exotic fruit?
2.2 Fruits.explanation: Mangoes are exotic (originally from other countries) but grown locally.

2.2 Fruits.q: Crops that bear their seeds in pods and have root nodules are
2.2 Fruits.explanation: Legumes bear seeds in pods and have nitrogen-fixing root nodules.

2.2 Fruits.q: Legumes are an especially good source of
2.2 Fruits.explanation: Legume seeds are a very good source of proteins.
Cereal grains contain a lot of carbohydrates, giving high energy.

2.2 Fruits.q: Which of the following is a cereal crop?
2.2 Fruits.explanation: Maize is a cereal; the others are legumes.

2.2 Fruits.q: Oranges are a good source of
2.2 Fruits.explanation: Citrus fruits such as oranges are rich in Vitamin C.

2.2 Fruits.q: Fibre obtained from fruits helps the body by
2.2 Fruits.explanation: Fibre helps digestion and prevents constipation.

3.1 Separating Substances.q: A solid that dissolves in a liquid is said to be
3.1 Separating Substances.explanation: A soluble solid dissolves in a liquid.

3.1 Separating Substances.q: The liquid in which a solid dissolves is called the
3.1 Separating Substances.explanation: The solvent is the liquid in which the solute dissolves.

3.1 Separating Substances.q: The solid that dissolves to form a solution is the
3.1 Separating Substances.explanation: The solute is the solid that dissolves.

3.1 Separating Substances.q: Sand can be separated from water by
3.1 Separating Substances.explanation: Sand is insoluble; it settles and the water is decanted (poured off). Filtration also works.

3.1 Separating Substances.q: The best method to recover salt from salty water is
3.1 Separating Substances.explanation: Salt is soluble; heating the solution evaporates the water and leaves the salt.

3.1 Separating Substances.q: Iron filings can be separated from sand using a
3.1 Separating Substances.explanation: A magnet attracts the magnetic iron filings and leaves the sand.

3.1 Separating Substances.q: Which of the following is a magnetic material?
3.1 Separating Substances.explanation: Iron nails are magnetic; the others are non-magnetic.

3.1 Separating Substances.q: In filtration, the solid left behind on the filter is called the
3.1 Separating Substances.explanation: The solid trapped by the filter is the residue.

3.1 Separating Substances.q: Salt is an example of a
3.1 Separating Substances.explanation: Salt dissolves in water, so it is a soluble solid.

3.1 Separating Substances.q: To separate office pins from flour you would use a
3.1 Separating Substances.explanation: Pins are magnetic, so a magnet picks them out of the flour.

3.2 Water Supply Systems.q: Most of the water on Earth is
3.2 Water Supply Systems.explanation: About 97% of the Earth's water is salty (in seas and oceans).

3.2 Water Supply Systems.q: An underground source of water is a
3.2 Water Supply Systems.explanation: Wells, boreholes and springs are underground water sources.

3.2 Water Supply Systems.q: Before water reaches homes in town it must be
3.2 Water Supply Systems.explanation: Town water must be treated to make it safe for use.

3.2 Water Supply Systems.q: Which method of treating water kills germs but does not remove chemicals?
3.2 Water Supply Systems.explanation: Boiling kills germs but does not remove dissolved poisonous chemicals.

3.2 Water Supply Systems.q: The treatment method that removes chemicals, germs and even useful minerals is
3.2 Water Supply Systems.explanation: Distillation boils water and collects the pure vapour, removing chemicals, germs and minerals.

3.2 Water Supply Systems.q: Treating water by adding chlorine products such as waterguard is called
3.2 Water Supply Systems.explanation: Chlorination uses chlorine-containing products to treat water.

3.2 Water Supply Systems.q: The first stage in urban water treatment is
3.2 Water Supply Systems.explanation: Treatment begins with pumping water from the source.

3.2 Water Supply Systems.q: At the coagulation and flocculation stage, chemicals are added to
3.2 Water Supply Systems.explanation: Coagulation and flocculation make suspended particles clump so they can settle.

3.2 Water Supply Systems.q: Fluoride is added to treated water to
3.2 Water Supply Systems.explanation: Fluoride is added during neutralising to prevent tooth decay.

3.2 Water Supply Systems.q: Treating used water from sewage or factories so it can be used again is called
3.2 Water Supply Systems.explanation: Water recycling removes wastes and germs from used water so it can be used again.

3.2 Water Supply Systems.q: Covering soil around plants with dry grass or leaves to reduce evaporation is
3.2 Water Supply Systems.explanation: Mulching covers the soil to reduce loss of moisture by evaporation.

3.2 Water Supply Systems.q: Which statement best describes water conservation?
options: Preventing loss, wastage and pollution of water
3.2 Water Supply Systems.explanation: Water conservation means preventing water from being lost, wasted or polluted.

4.1 The Flower.q: A flower is the ___ organ of a plant.
4.1 The Flower.explanation: The flower is the reproductive organ of a plant.

4.1 The Flower.q: The male parts of a flower are together called the
4.1 The Flower.explanation: The stamen is made up of the anthers, filaments and pollen grains.

4.1 The Flower.q: The female parts of a flower are together called the
4.1 The Flower.explanation: The pistil is made up of the stigma, style, ovary and ovules.

4.1 The Flower.q: Which of the following is a male part of a flower?
4.1 The Flower.explanation: The anther is a male part; it produces pollen grains.

4.1 The Flower.q: Pollen grains are produced by the
The part that receives pollen grains during pollination is the
4.1 The Flower.explanation: The stigma receives pollen grains during pollination.
The ovary produces and contains the ovules.

4.1 The Flower.q: A group of petals is called the
4.1 The Flower.explanation: A collection of petals is called the corolla.

4.1 The Flower.q: A group of sepals is called the
4.1 The Flower.explanation: A group of sepals is called the calyx.

4.1 The Flower.q: Brightly coloured petals mainly help to
4.1 The Flower.explanation: Bright petals attract insects (and birds) that help in pollination.

4.1 The Flower.q: Study the diagram of a flower. Which labelled part develops into a fruit after fertilisation?
4.1 The Flower.explanation: After fertilisation the ovary develops into a fruit.

4.1 The Flower.q: In the flower shown, the part that produces the male reproductive cells (pollen) is the
4.1 The Flower.explanation: The anther produces pollen grains, the male reproductive cells.

4.2 Pollination and Fertilisation.q: Pollination is the transfer of pollen grains from the anther to the
4.2 Pollination and Fertilisation.explanation: Pollination is the transfer of pollen from the anther to the stigma.

4.2 Pollination and Fertilisation.q: The transfer of pollen to the stigma of the same flower is called
4.2 Pollination and Fertilisation.explanation: Self-pollination involves the same flower (or same plant).

4.2 Pollination and Fertilisation.q: The transfer of pollen to a different plant of the same kind is called
4.2 Pollination and Fertilisation.explanation: Cross-pollination is between different plants of the same kind.

4.2 Pollination and Fertilisation.q: Which of the following is an agent of pollination?
4.2 Pollination and Fertilisation.explanation: Wind, water and animals are agents of pollination.

4.2 Pollination and Fertilisation.q: Insects are attracted to flowers mainly by the
4.2 Pollination and Fertilisation.explanation: Bright petals, scent and nectar attract insects to flowers.

4.2 Pollination and Fertilisation.q: Fertilisation is the joining of a pollen grain and an
4.2 Pollination and Fertilisation.explanation: Fertilisation is the fusion of a pollen grain and an ovule to form a zygote.

4.2 Pollination and Fertilisation.q: Fertilisation in a plant takes place in the
4.2 Pollination and Fertilisation.explanation: Fertilisation takes place in the ovary.

4.2 Pollination and Fertilisation.q: After pollination, the pollen tube grows down towards the ovary through the
4.2 Pollination and Fertilisation.explanation: The pollen tube grows through the style to reach the ovary.

4.2 Pollination and Fertilisation.q: After fertilisation, an ovule becomes a
4.2 Pollination and Fertilisation.explanation: Each fertilised ovule develops into a seed.

4.2 Pollination and Fertilisation.q: Which part does NOT fall off after fertilisation?
4.2 Pollination and Fertilisation.explanation: The ovary remains and becomes the fruit; petals, anthers, stigma, style and filament fall off.

4.3 Fruits and Seeds.q: After fertilisation, the ovary becomes a
4.3 Fruits and Seeds.explanation: The ovary develops into a fruit after fertilisation.

4.3 Fruits and Seeds.q: A seed develops from a fertilised
The outer covering of a seed is called the
4.3 Fruits and Seeds.explanation: The seed coat is called the testa (the fruit covering is the pericarp).

4.3 Fruits and Seeds.q: A fruit usually has ___ scars while a seed has one.
4.3 Fruits and Seeds.explanation: Fruits have two scars while seeds have only one scar.

4.3 Fruits and Seeds.q: Compared with seeds, most fruits are
4.3 Fruits and Seeds.explanation: Most fruits are juicy, while most seeds are dry.

4.3 Fruits and Seeds.q: Plants produce many seeds mainly to
options: make sure some grow into new plants
4.3 Fruits and Seeds.explanation: Producing many seeds ensures that some will find favourable conditions and grow.

4.3 Fruits and Seeds.q: The production of new plants is called plant
4.3 Fruits and Seeds.explanation: Plant propagation is the production of new plants.

4.3 Fruits and Seeds.q: An improved seed variety is one that
options: gives higher yields than the original
4.3 Fruits and Seeds.explanation: Improved varieties give higher yields and other benefits.

4.3 Fruits and Seeds.q: Which of the following is a benefit of improved seed varieties?
4.3 Fruits and Seeds.explanation: Improved seeds can resist drought and disease, mature early and give higher yields.

4.3 Fruits and Seeds.q: Seeds are important to plants because they
options: protect and continue the plant species
4.3 Fruits and Seeds.explanation: Seeds propagate the next generation and stop a species from becoming extinct.

options: moving of seeds away from the parent plant
transfer of pollen to the stigma
making of food by the plant
4.4 Seed Dispersal.explanation: Seed dispersal is moving seeds away from the parent plant.

4.4 Seed Dispersal.q: Seeds that are dispersed by wind are usually
4.4 Seed Dispersal.explanation: Wind-dispersed seeds are small and light, sometimes with wings or hairs.

4.4 Seed Dispersal.q: Jacaranda seeds are mainly dispersed by
4.4 Seed Dispersal.explanation: Jacaranda seeds have wing-like structures and are dispersed by wind.
Coconuts are light and have air spaces, so they float and are dispersed by water.

4.4 Seed Dispersal.q: Seeds with hooks, such as the black jack, are dispersed by
4.4 Seed Dispersal.explanation: Hooked seeds attach to the fur of animals and are carried away.

4.4 Seed Dispersal.q: When a dry pod bursts open to throw out its seeds, this is
4.4 Seed Dispersal.explanation: Self-dispersal is when pods burst open to release seeds.

4.4 Seed Dispersal.q: Which of the following seeds are dispersed by explosion?
4.4 Seed Dispersal.explanation: Peas and beans have pods that burst open (explosive dispersal).

4.4 Seed Dispersal.q: A guava eaten by a bird and its seeds dropped far away is an example of dispersal by
4.4 Seed Dispersal.explanation: Animals eat fruits and pass or drop the seeds far from the parent plant.

4.4 Seed Dispersal.q: Seeds that float and contain air spaces are dispersed by
4.4 Seed Dispersal.explanation: Air spaces help seeds float, so they are dispersed by water.

4.4 Seed Dispersal.q: Seed dispersal is important because it
4.4 Seed Dispersal.explanation: Dispersal spreads seeds out, reducing competition for light, water and nutrients.

4.5 Propagation.q: Growing new plants from parts of an existing plant is called ___ propagation.
4.5 Propagation.explanation: Vegetative propagation uses parts of an existing plant.

4.5 Propagation.q: Planting seeds in lines is called
4.5 Propagation.explanation: Row planting places seeds in rows, e.g. maize and beans.

4.5 Propagation.q: Scattering seeds over a field is called
4.5 Propagation.explanation: Broadcasting scatters seeds, which are then covered with soil.

4.5 Propagation.q: Millet and sorghum are usually propagated by
4.5 Propagation.explanation: Millet and sorghum are commonly broadcast.

4.5 Propagation.q: Cassava and sugarcane are commonly grown from
4.5 Propagation.explanation: These plants are grown from stem cuttings.

4.5 Propagation.q: Joining a twig (scion) onto the stem of another plant of the same type is called
4.5 Propagation.explanation: Grafting joins a scion to a stock.

4.5 Propagation.q: In grafting, the rooted plant onto which the scion is joined is called the
4.5 Propagation.explanation: The stock is the rooted plant that receives the scion.

4.5 Propagation.q: Pegging a branch into the soil so it grows roots, then cutting it off, is called
4.5 Propagation.explanation: Layering (marcotting) induces roots on a branch still attached to the parent.

4.5 Propagation.q: Small plantlets that grow beside a banana plant are called
4.5 Propagation.explanation: Suckers are plantlets that grow beside the parent and can be separated.

options: improve the quality and yield of a plant
4.5 Propagation.explanation: Grafting improves the quality and yield of a plant that was formerly poor.

5.1 Energy.explanation: Energy is the ability to do work.

5.1 Energy.q: The energy in a moving object is called
5.1 Energy.explanation: Kinetic energy is the energy of a moving object.

5.1 Energy.q: Energy stored in an object because of its position or state is called
5.1 Energy.explanation: Potential energy is stored energy due to position or state.

5.1 Energy.q: The energy stored in fuels and food is
5.1 Energy.explanation: Fuels and food store chemical energy, released when burnt or used.

5.1 Energy.q: Sound energy is produced when objects
The main natural source of light and heat energy is the
5.1 Energy.explanation: The Sun is the main source of light and heat energy.

5.1 Energy.q: Which of the following is a natural source of light?
5.1 Energy.explanation: Fireflies are natural sources of light; candles, torches and bulbs are artificial.

5.1 Energy.q: Which of the following is an artificial source of light?
5.1 Energy.explanation: A torch is an artificial (man-made) source of light.

5.1 Energy.q: The law of conservation of energy states that energy can be
options: changed from one form to another
5.1 Energy.explanation: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only changed from one form to another.

5.1 Energy.q: In a torch, chemical energy is finally changed into
5.1 Energy.explanation: Chemical to electrical to heat, and finally to light in the bulb.

5.1 Energy.q: When wood burns, the energy change is
5.1 Energy.explanation: Burning wood changes chemical energy into heat and then light.

5.1 Energy.q: A stretched rubber band has ___ energy.
5.1 Energy.explanation: A stretched rubber band stores potential energy.

5.2 Electric Current and Circuits.q: An electric current is the flow of
5.2 Electric Current and Circuits.explanation: An electric current is the flow of charges (electrons) in a conductor.

5.2 Electric Current and Circuits.q: Current flows from the ___ terminal to the negative terminal of a cell.
5.2 Electric Current and Circuits.explanation: Current flows from the positive to the negative terminal of a cell.

5.2 Electric Current and Circuits.q: For an electric current to flow, the circuit must be
5.2 Electric Current and Circuits.explanation: Current only flows when the circuit is complete.

5.2 Electric Current and Circuits.q: Which part is the source of energy in a simple circuit?
5.2 Electric Current and Circuits.explanation: The dry cell (which stores chemical energy) is the source of electrical energy.

5.2 Electric Current and Circuits.q: A bulb changes electrical energy mainly into
5.2 Electric Current and Circuits.explanation: The bulb converts electrical energy into heat and light.

5.2 Electric Current and Circuits.q: The part used to open or close a circuit is the
5.2 Electric Current and Circuits.explanation: A switch opens or closes the circuit to control the current.

5.2 Electric Current and Circuits.q: When a switch is open, the bulb
5.2 Electric Current and Circuits.explanation: An open switch leaves a gap, so the circuit is incomplete and the bulb does not light.

5.2 Electric Current and Circuits.q: In a series circuit, if one bulb is removed the other bulbs
5.2 Electric Current and Circuits.explanation: In series, removing one bulb breaks the circuit and the others go off.

5.2 Electric Current and Circuits.q: Bulbs that share the cell's voltage and light dimly are connected in
5.2 Electric Current and Circuits.explanation: Bulbs in series share the voltage and light dimly.

5.2 Electric Current and Circuits.q: Bulbs that each light at full brightness, and stay on if one fails, are connected in
5.2 Electric Current and Circuits.explanation: In parallel, each bulb lights fully and the others stay on if one fails.

5.2 Electric Current and Circuits.q: Study the circuit shown. The bulbs are connected in
5.2 Electric Current and Circuits.explanation: The three bulbs are in one line (one loop), so they are in series.

5.2 Electric Current and Circuits.q: Study the circuit shown. The bulbs are connected in
5.2 Electric Current and Circuits.explanation: Each bulb is on its own branch (separate line), so they are in parallel.

5.3 Lightning.explanation: Lightning is a result of static electricity that builds up in clouds.

5.3 Lightning.q: Lightning is a flash of bright light caused by an electric
5.3 Lightning.explanation: Lightning is a flash of bright light caused by an electric spark.

5.3 Lightning.q: Lightning usually strikes the ___ objects on its path.
5.3 Lightning.explanation: Lightning strikes the tallest objects, such as tall trees and buildings.

5.3 Lightning.q: Lightning is a major cause of
5.3 Lightning.explanation: Lightning striking trees is a major cause of forest fires.

5.3 Lightning.q: The device fitted on tall buildings to carry lightning safely to the ground is a
5.3 Lightning.explanation: A lightning arrester (Franklin's rod) conducts the charge to the ground.

5.3 Lightning.q: A lightning arrester is made of thick
5.3 Lightning.explanation: A lightning arrester is a thick copper wire/rod.

options: wear shoes with thick rubber soles
5.3 Lightning.explanation: Thick rubber soles help protect you; the other actions are dangerous.

5.3 Lightning.q: Which of the following should you avoid during a thunderstorm?
5.3 Lightning.explanation: You should not use the phone during a thunderstorm.

5.3 Lightning.q: In farming, lightning is useful because it
options: fixes nitrogen in the air into nitrates in the soil
5.3 Lightning.explanation: Lightning breaks nitrogen into nitrates that enrich the soil.

5.3 Lightning.q: Plants use the nitrates formed by lightning to make
5.3 Lightning.explanation: Plants use nitrates to make proteins for healthy growth.

5.4 The Solar System.q: At the centre of the solar system is the
5.4 The Solar System.explanation: The Sun is at the centre of the solar system.
The Sun is a star that produces its own light and heat.

5.4 The Solar System.q: An object that produces its own light is said to be
5.4 The Solar System.explanation: A luminous object produces its own light, like the Sun.

5.4 The Solar System.q: We can see the planets because they
5.4 The Solar System.explanation: Planets are non-luminous; they reflect the Sun's light.

5.4 The Solar System.q: The planet closest to the Sun is
5.4 The Solar System.explanation: Mercury is the closest planet to the Sun.

5.4 The Solar System.q: The largest planet in the solar system is
The only planet known to support life is
5.4 The Solar System.explanation: Earth is the only planet known to support life.

5.4 The Solar System.q: Day and night are caused by the Earth
5.4 The Solar System.explanation: Day and night are caused by the Earth spinning on its axis every 24 hours.

5.4 The Solar System.q: The seasons are caused by the
options: distance of the Earth from the Sun
5.4 The Solar System.explanation: The tilt of the Earth's axis causes the seasons as it revolves round the Sun.

5.4 The Solar System.q: The Earth takes about ___ to revolve once round the Sun.
5.4 The Solar System.explanation: The Earth takes about 365 1/4 days (one year) to orbit the Sun.

5.4 The Solar System.q: The Moon takes about ___ to orbit the Earth.
5.4 The Solar System.explanation: The Moon takes about 28 days (one month) to orbit the Earth.

5.4 The Solar System.q: A solar eclipse happens when the ___ comes between the Sun and the Earth.
5.4 The Solar System.explanation: In a solar eclipse the Moon comes between the Sun and the Earth.

5.4 The Solar System.q: Study the diagram. It shows the formation of a
5.4 The Solar System.explanation: The Moon is between the Sun and the Earth, blocking the Sun's light: a solar eclipse.

5.4 The Solar System.q: The total (dark) shadow formed during an eclipse is called the
5.4 The Solar System.explanation: The umbra is the total shadow; the penumbra is the partial shadow.

5.5 Metals and Non-metals.explanation: Most metals are good conductors of electricity and heat.
Most non-metals do not conduct electricity; they are insulators.

5.5 Metals and Non-metals.q: The only metal that is liquid at room temperature is
5.5 Metals and Non-metals.explanation: Mercury is the only metal that is liquid at room temperature.

5.5 Metals and Non-metals.q: The best conductor of electricity is
5.5 Metals and Non-metals.explanation: Silver is the best conductor of electricity, followed by copper.

5.5 Metals and Non-metals.q: A substance made from a mixture of two or more metals is called an
5.5 Metals and Non-metals.explanation: An alloy is a mixture of two or more metals (or a metal with another element).

5.5 Metals and Non-metals.q: Brass is an alloy of copper and
5.5 Metals and Non-metals.explanation: Brass is a mixture of copper and zinc (bronze is copper and tin).

5.5 Metals and Non-metals.q: Which metal is light but strong and is used for aeroplane bodies?
5.5 Metals and Non-metals.explanation: Aluminium is light and strong, so it is used for aeroplane bodies.

5.5 Metals and Non-metals.q: Iron is coated (galvanised) with ___ to prevent it from rusting.
5.5 Metals and Non-metals.explanation: Zinc is used to coat iron to prevent rusting.

5.5 Metals and Non-metals.q: Which of the following is a non-metal?
5.5 Metals and Non-metals.explanation: Oxygen is a non-metal; the others are metals.

5.5 Metals and Non-metals.q: Glass is used for window panes because it is
5.5 Metals and Non-metals.explanation: Glass is transparent and a poor conductor of heat and electricity.

5.6 Mining.q: Mining is the process of getting ___ from underground.
5.6 Mining.explanation: Mining is the process of getting mineral ores from underground.

5.6 Mining.q: A rock that contains minerals mixed with impurities is called an
5.6 Mining.explanation: An ore is a rock that contains minerals together with impurities.

5.6 Mining.q: Ores found deep in the earth are mined by the ___ method.
5.6 Mining.explanation: Deep ores are mined using the shaft method.

5.6 Mining.q: The most commonly mined mineral in Zambia is
5.6 Mining.explanation: Copper is the most commonly mined mineral and the highest export earner.

5.6 Mining.q: Copper in Zambia is mainly mined on the
5.6 Mining.explanation: Most copper is mined in the Copperbelt towns.

5.6 Mining.q: Which of the following is a property of copper?
5.6 Mining.explanation: Copper is a very good conductor of electricity.

5.6 Mining.q: The three stages of copper extraction are crushing, floatation and
5.6 Mining.explanation: The three extraction stages are crushing, floatation and smelting.

5.6 Mining.q: The last process that gives 100% pure copper is
5.6 Mining.explanation: Refining (by electrolysis) removes the last impurities to give pure copper.

5.6 Mining.q: Copper is refined using a process called
5.6 Mining.explanation: Refining of copper is done by electrolysis.

5.6 Mining.q: Which of the following minerals is NOT mined in Zambia?
5.6 Mining.explanation: Diamond is not among the minerals mined in Zambia.

5.6 Mining.q: A harmful effect of mining on the environment is
options: pollution of air, land and water
5.6 Mining.explanation: Mining pollutes air, land and water; the others are benefits.

5.6 Mining.q: Study the map of Zambia. Copper is mostly mined in the province named
5.6 Mining.explanation: The map and key show copper is mined mainly in the Copperbelt Province.

1.1 What is Democracy?.explanation: Democracy means rule by the people.

1.1 What is Democracy?.q: A party with fewer votes than the ruling party is called the

1.1 What is Democracy?.q: Which document lists the rights every person should have?
options: The Universal Declaration of Human Rights
1.1 What is Democracy?.explanation: The UN proclaimed the Universal Declaration of Human Rights in 1948.

1.1 What is Democracy?.q: Treating people unequally and unfairly is called
Forcing children to work instead of going to school is called
1.1 What is Democracy?.explanation: Child labour is forcing children to work without proper pay.

1.1 What is Democracy?.q: Laws passed by the government and written down are called
1.1 What is Democracy?.explanation: Statutory laws are written laws passed by government.

1.2 How Government Works.q: Which branch of government makes laws?

The branch that makes sure people obey the law is the

1.2 How Government Works.explanation: The Supreme Court is the highest; its decisions are final.

1.2 How Government Works.q: Asking a higher court to hear a case again is called an
1.2 How Government Works.explanation: An appeal is asking a higher court to rule on a case.

1.2 How Government Works.q: Why must the branches of government stay separate?
options: So one branch does not get too much power
So the president can rule alone
1.2 How Government Works.explanation: Separation of powers stops any branch having too much power.

1.2 How Government Works.q: Teachers, nurses and police officers are part of the
Parliament is made up of the National Assembly and the
1.2 How Government Works.explanation: Parliament = National Assembly and the president.

1.3 What Government Does.q: The head of government is the
1.3 What Government Does.explanation: The president is the head of government.

1.3 What Government Does.q: Money the government collects, mainly from taxes, is called
1.3 What Government Does.explanation: Tax money collected is called revenue.

1.3 What Government Does.q: Civil servants' salaries are paid from
Who stands in for the president when he or she is away?
1.3 What Government Does.explanation: The vice-president stands in for the president.

1.3 What Government Does.q: Which ministry decides how to spend the government's revenue?
1.3 What Government Does.explanation: The Ministry of Finance plans the spending.

1.3 What Government Does.q: Cabinet ministers are chosen by the
Which is an example of a service the government provides?
1.3 What Government Does.explanation: Government provides services such as hospitals and schools.

1.4 Government Ministries.explanation: A ministry is a national department run by a minister.

1.4 Government Ministries.q: Which ministry issues passports and national registration cards?
1.4 Government Ministries.explanation: Home Affairs keeps records and issues passports and NRCs.

options: a set of written rules for a country

1.5 The Constitution.explanation: The constitution is the supreme (most important) law.

1.5 The Constitution.explanation: General elections are held every five years.

1.5 The Constitution.explanation: A by-election replaces a leader who dies, steps down or loses their job.

1.5 The Constitution.explanation: A two-thirds majority of parliament is needed.

1.5 The Constitution.explanation: A referendum is held when a change affects citizens' rights.

1.5 The Constitution.explanation: Zambia is a Christian country but everyone may choose their religion.

2.1 The Continents.q: Which continent has the most people?

2.1 The Continents.explanation: Antarctica is the coldest and least populated.

2.1 The Continents.explanation: The equator passes through South America (and Africa).

2.1 The Continents.q: The longest mountain range in the world, the Andes, is in
2.1 The Continents.explanation: The Andes, the longest range, are in South America.

2.1 The Continents.q: About what percentage of the world's land is Africa's neighbour Asia?
2.1 The Continents.explanation: Asia covers about 30% of the Earth's land.

2.2 Physical Features of the World.q: A large area of water surrounded by land is a
2.2 Physical Features of the World.explanation: A lake is water surrounded by land.

2.2 Physical Features of the World.explanation: Lake Victoria is Africa's largest lake.

2.2 Physical Features of the World.explanation: Most mountains are fold mountains formed by shifting continents.

2.2 Physical Features of the World.explanation: A plateau is high, flat land (a tableland).

2.2 Physical Features of the World.explanation: Deserts make up about one-third of the Earth.

2.2 Physical Features of the World.explanation: The Great Rift Valley runs through East Africa.

3.1 Population Growth.q: The number of births per thousand people per year is the
3.1 Population Growth.explanation: The birth rate is births per thousand per year.

3.1 Population Growth.q: Which is a reason for rapid population growth?
options: Better health care so people live longer
3.1 Population Growth.explanation: Better health care lowers the death rate, so population grows.

3.1 Population Growth.q: Too many people for the available resources is called
3.1 Population Growth.explanation: Overpopulation is too many people for the resources.

3.1 Population Growth.q: Overcrowded poor settlements that grow in cities are called
3.1 Population Growth.explanation: Rapid growth leads to slums or shanty compounds.

3.1 Population Growth.q: Which helps to solve rapid population growth?
3.1 Population Growth.explanation: Family planning and education help control growth.

3.1 Population Growth.q: When a country's economy moves from farms to factories it is said to be

options: not enough jobs and more crime
3.1 Population Growth.explanation: Too-fast growth means too few jobs, more unemployment and crime.

options: dishonest behaviour by people in power
3.2 Corruption.explanation: Corruption is dishonest behaviour by people in power.

3.2 Corruption.explanation: Officials control public money, so people try to bribe them.

3.2 Corruption.explanation: Being accountable is taking responsibility for your actions.

3.3 Food Shortages.q: Making sure there is enough food now and in future is called

3.3 Food Shortages.explanation: Malnutrition is caused by lack of good food.

3.3 Food Shortages.explanation: A cash crop is grown to sell, not to eat.

3.3 Food Shortages.explanation: Droughts, floods and disasters cause food shortages.

options: Irrigating dry areas and using good farming

3.3 Food Shortages.explanation: A drought is a long period with little or no rain.

3.4 HIV and AIDS.explanation: AIDS is caused by HIV, the Human Immunodeficiency Virus.

options: Sick people cannot farm, so less food is grown

3.4 HIV and AIDS.q: HIV and AIDS is described as a

options: harmful substances that make the environment dirty
3.5 Pollution.explanation: Pollution is harmful substances that dirty the environment.

3.6 Other World Challenges.explanation: Melting ice raises sea levels and floods low areas.

3.6 Other World Challenges.explanation: Education is the key to a well-paid job and ending poverty.

3.6 Other World Challenges.explanation: The UN (and AU) help fighting groups make peace.

4.1 Family Ties.explanation: Families give security, protection, love and care.

5.1 The Prairies.explanation: The root-matted soil needed strong steel ploughs.

5.4 The Steppes.explanation: The Steppes are dry, cold grasslands.

5.4 The Steppes.explanation: Poor soil and a cold, dry climate limit farming.

5.4 The Steppes.explanation: Being far from the ocean and near mountains makes the air dry.

5.5 The Veld.explanation: The highveld is used for cereals, livestock and market gardening.

6.1 Building Africa Together.q: COMESA stands for the Common Market for

6.1 Building Africa Together.explanation: A free trade zone removes customs controls and taxes.

6.2 Transport Systems.explanation: Air transport is quickest but most expensive.
